Serarius suspects that in place of dirigeret one should read dirigaret, that is, might irrigate; but dirigeret fits this passage clearly enough. For a spring directs and conveys water into the city through a channel.
The sons of Ammon and Moab came to Holofernes.] The Greek texts have: The princes of the Edomites and the Moabites. For the Edomites, invading the Jews...

These men therefore counseled Holofernes to seize the springs, and thus to reduce the Jews by thirst. # GEM Mystically: the devil cuts off the aqueduct when he causes the pursuit of literary studies to be interrupted. For not far from the walls were springs — the doctrines of the Philosophers — which afford a certain comfort of knowledge and honorable conduct, from which they seemed to draw water for refreshment rather than for drinking.

Judith is described as the daughter of Merari, son of Idox, son of Joseph, etc., tracing her lineage back to Reuben. The text recounts her long genealogy to establish her noble Israelite descent and credibility.
Her husband was Manasses, who died during the barley harvest.
He had been standing over those who bound sheaves in the field, and the heat came upon his head, and he died in Bethulia his city and was buried there with his fathers.
Judith had now been a widow for three years and six months.
In the upper part of her house she had made for herself a private chamber, in which she dwelt shut away with her maidens.
Wearing sackcloth upon her loins, she fasted all the days of her life, except on sabbaths, new moons, and feasts of the house of Israel.
She was of very elegant appearance, and her husband had left her great wealth, a large household, and possessions full of herds of oxen and flocks of sheep.
She was most renowned among all, because she feared the Lord greatly, and there was no one who spoke an evil word of her.
When she heard that Ozias had promised to hand over the city after five days had passed, she sent for the elders Chabri and Charmi.
[Commentary continues on Judith summoning the elders to rebuke their decision.]

DEFECERUNT CISTERNAE ET COLLECTIONES AQUARUM. The Greek adds: And their children were dismayed, and the young men fainted from thirst and fell in the streets of the city and in the gateways, and there was no longer any strength in them. # GEM

TUNC AD OZIAM CONGREGATI. The people, exhausted by thirst, gathered demanding with loud cries that Bethulia be surrendered to Holofernes. Nothing more dreadful befalls the besieged than thirst; for thirst torments far more acutely than hunger. Hence generals besieging a city are wont to divert the water supply so as to wear down the citizens with thirst and compel them to surrender, according to the text: 'The tongue of the suckling cleaveth to the roof of his mouth for thirst' (Lam. 4). Well known is the story of Lysimachus king of the Macedonians, who, when he was suffering from lack of water and thirst in Thrace, surrendered himself and his army to the enemy; but after drinking water he cried out: 'O gods! for how small a pleasure have I made myself a slave instead of a king!' The same may be said of the covetous, who for a moment's pleasure make themselves slaves of the infernal Holofernes, that is, the devil, who will torment them in hell with thirst, so that they cry out with the rich man: 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue; for I am tormented in this flame' (Luke 16). # GEM
FACTUS EST FLETUS ET ULULATUS MAGNUS IN ECCLESIA. In Greek: in the synagogue, that is, in the place of prayer.

JUDITH THE WIDOW. ] Some think that Judith is a common name, as Abra is, but appropriated to this heroine: for יְהוּדִי Jehudi means the same as Judaean; whence יְהוּדִית Jekudit, and by crasis, Judith, means the same as Judaean.

More truly, Judith is a proper name, whose etymology aptly fits here. For Judith in Hebrew means the same as confessing, praising, glorifying — namely God; for הוֹדָה hoda means to confess, to praise, to celebrate. For this purpose Judith withdrew from the crowd into her oratory, to pray and praise God continually.

# GEM Hear St. Fulgentius, Epistle 2, on the widowed state, citing these words about Judith: "Behold a widow, illustrious by birth, wealthy in resources, young in age, lovable in beauty — she despised riches, rejected delights, trampled the incentives of the flesh, and clothed in virtue sought not to serve a second marriage. Therefore by the testimony of so distinguished a work it appeared how greatly widowed continence is beloved by God."

DAUGHTER OF MERARI, SON OF IDOX. ] Note: Only sixteen generations and as many ancestors of Judith are enumerated here, whereas from Jacob the common patriarch of the Jews down to the Babylonian captivity, which preceded the age of Judith, 26 generations are counted, as in Matthew 1. One of two things must therefore be said here: either not all of Judith's ancestors are enumerated here, just as neither are those of Eleazar

Then Judith heard the harsh words which the people spoke against the ruler, because their spirit had failed on account of want of water; and Judith heard all the words that Ozias spoke to them, and how he swore to surrender the city to the Assyrians after five days. And sending her handmaid, who was over all her possessions, she called Ozias and Chabri and Charmis, the elders of the city.
When they came to her, she said to them: What is this word, in which Ozias consented, to deliver the city to the Assyrians, if within five days help should not come to you?
And who are you, who tempt the Lord?
This is not a word that draws forth mercy, but rather one that stirs up wrath and kindles fury.
You have set a time for the compassion of the Lord, and according to your own judgment you have appointed him a day.
But because the Lord is patient, in this very thing let us repent, and with tears poured forth let us beg his indulgence;
for God will not threaten as a man does, nor will he be inflamed to anger as the son of man.
And therefore let us humble our souls before him, and continuing in a humbled spirit, let us serve him.
Let us say weeping to the Lord, that according to his will he may show us his mercy: that as our heart has been troubled by their pride, so also we may glory in our own humiliation;
because we have not followed the sins of our fathers, who forsook their God and worshipped strange gods.
For which crime they were given to the sword, to plunder, and to confusion before their enemies; but we know no other God but him.

Let us humbly wait for his consolation, and he will avenge our blood from the afflictions of our enemies, and he will humble all the nations that rise up against us, and the Lord our God will make them without honour.

And now, brethren, since you are the elders among the people of God, and their very souls depend upon you, lift up their hearts by your speech, that they may be mindful that our fathers were tempted that they might be proved, whether they truly worshipped their God.
They ought to be mindful of how our father Abraham was tempted, and being proved through many tribulations, was made the friend of God.
So Isaac, so Jacob, so Moses, and all who pleased God, passed through many tribulations remaining faithful.
But those who received not the temptations with the fear of the Lord, and who vented their impatience and the reproach of their murmuring against the Lord,
were destroyed by the destroyer and perished by serpents.
And as for us, therefore, let us not revenge ourselves for these things which we suffer;

but reckoning these very punishments to be less than our sins, let us believe that these scourges of the Lord, by which like servants we are chastised, have come for our amendment, and not for our destruction.
And Ozias and the elders said to her: All things which you have spoken are true, and there is nothing to be reproved in your words.
Now therefore pray for us, for you are a holy woman and one fearing God.
And Judith said to them: As you acknowledge that what I have been able to say is from God,
so also prove whether what I purpose to do is of God, and pray that God may strengthen my counsel.
Stand at the gate this night, and I will go out with my handmaid; and pray that, as you have said, within five days the Lord may look upon his people Israel.
But I will not have you inquire into my act, and until I bring you word let nothing else be done but prayer for me to the Lord our God.
And Ozias the prince of Judah said to her: Go in peace, and the Lord be with you to take vengeance on our enemies. And returning they went away.

# GEM S. Scripture suffices to trace a genealogy up to some illustrious person, e.g., in the case of Saul, to trace it back to Benjamin. Or certainly the grandfathers and great-grandfathers of Judah begot sons in old age. For this reason, from Ezra back to Aaron, sixteen generations are counted in 1 Esdras ch. 7 v. 2; whereas in 1 Paralipomenon 6:3, from Jozadak, who was a contemporary and indeed brother of Ezra, back to Aaron, the generations are numbered...

Judith chapter 1 — Arphaxad king of the Medes built Ecbatana with great walls and towers, as is recounted here. Note that this Arphaxad is not the son of Shem mentioned in Genesis 10, but another king of the Medes who bore the same name, as is common among oriental peoples who repeat ancestral names across generations.

On the city of Ecbatana: This was the royal capital of the Medes, called by the Greeks Ecbatana, exceedingly famous for its wealth and fortifications. Herodotus, Diodorus, and others describe its seven concentric walls, each of a different color, the innermost plated with gold and silver.

The walls of Ecbatana were seventy cubits high and fifty cubits wide — dimensions that exceed all credibility unless we understand these as royal cubits, larger than ordinary ones, or unless the text intends to convey the impression of seemingly impregnable fortification rather than exact measurement.

Nebuchadnezzar king of Assyria — Note the difficulty: historically Nebuchadnezzar was king of Babylon, not Assyria. Various solutions are offered: (1) he ruled both kingdoms; (2) this is a different Nebuchadnezzar; (3) Assyria here is used loosely for the broader Mesopotamian empire.

The great plain of Ragau — This is identified with the region of Rages in Media, known from the book of Tobias. It was a broad fertile plain suitable for the great battle here described between Nebuchadnezzar and Arphaxad.

The nations despised the command of Nebuchadnezzar and did not go with him to war — This pride and contempt of the Gentile nations is here divinely permitted so that through Holofernes God might afterward chastise them, and through Judith deliver His own people, thus showing that human counsels avail nothing against divine providence. # GEM

Nebuchadnezzar swore by his throne and kingdom to take revenge — Note the impious oath of a pagan king who swears not by God but by his own power, revealing the blindness of idolaters who place their trust in earthly dominion rather than in the living God.

Arphaxad was defeated and slain by Nebuchadnezzar in the mountains of Ragau — Thus God permitted the proud Arphaxad, who had trusted in his massive fortifications and walls, to fall; for no walls of stone avail against the judgment of God. The mightiest fortresses crumble when God withdraws His protection. # GEM

Nebuchadnezzar returned to Nineveh with all his army — After the great victory he celebrated for many days, as conquerors do; but this very celebration and rest would become the occasion through which divine providence would bring about the humiliation of his general Holofernes at the hands of a widow.
The sons of Reuben have the sons of Israel, that is, of Jacob, and so S. Fulgentius Ep. 2, c. 24, and Bellarmine judge it should be read. # GEM

Others hold differently. Since, however, the Roman corrected codices and others consistently have *filii Ruben* ["son of Ruben"], it must be said that this Ruben is not the Patriarch and firstborn of Jacob, but rather an illustrious man from the tribe of Simeon, to whom accordingly the genealogy of Judith is here brought, and in whom it terminates, without proceeding further to his fathers and ancestors up to Simeon the Patriarch, the second son of Jacob. That this is so is clear from what has been said, and from the fact that Reuben the firstborn of Jacob had no son who was called Simeon, as is evident from Genesis 46:9 and 1 Chronicles 5:3. So Lyra.
*Vir ejus Manasses* ["Her husband Manasses"]: The Greek adds that he was from the same tribe and homeland as Judith.

*Instabat* ["He oversaw"]: In Greek *ephistemi*, that is, "he stood over"; whence some think Manasses was the overseer of the harvest, not his own private harvest, but the public one, that is, of the whole field of Bethulia. For so at Rome and elsewhere there are public overseers of grain and the food supply. So Josephus in his own Life says that command of the great plain was entrusted to Arbutius.
*Sex mensibus* ["Six months"]: The Greek has four months.

*Et in superioribus domus suae* (that is, in the upper floor or solar) *fecit sibi secretum cubiculum, in quo cum puellis* (handmaids) *suis clausa morabatur* ["And in the upper part of her house she made herself a secret chamber, in which she lived shut away with her maidservants"]: both in order to flee men and the crowds of visitors, and thereby to provide for her chastity and reputation; and also that she might give herself wholly to prayer and to God. This therefore is the first mark of Judith's widowhood, namely the love of silence and solitude. Hence St. Jerome, in his letter to the widow Furia, gives her this counsel: *Flee the company of young men; do not go out in public, and be carried about with the freedom of widows escorted by a troop of eunuchs; seek the company of holy virgins and widows. And if necessity compels you to speak with men, do not avoid witnesses, and let your confidence in conversation be such that when another enters you neither tremble nor blush. The face is the mirror of the mind, and the silent eyes confess the secrets of the heart.* And further: *Redeem virgins whom you may lead into the chamber of the King; receive widows whom you may mingle among the lilies of virgins and the roses of martyrs, as certain violets; in place of the crown of thorns in which Christ bore the sins of the world, weave such garlands.* # GEM

*Et habens super lumbos suos cilicium* ["And wearing a hairshirt upon her loins"]: Continually pricking and stinging the flesh with its bristles as with needles, and by that pain suppressing and extinguishing the impulses of base pleasure that boil up with youthful blood. For the hairshirt tames these more effectively than the passing pains and stripes of whips, because it is unceasing and continuous. Therefore young people, especially widowed women, should arm themselves with the hairshirt as a most powerful defense for taming the flesh and preserving chastity. See our Gretser, book On Disciplines; for as St. Jerome says, Epistle 11 to the widow Ageruchia: *Certainly it is far more laborious not to enjoy what you possess than to desire what you have lost. Whence virginity is easier in that it knows not the incentives of the flesh, while widowhood is more anxious in that the mind recalls past pleasures.* # GEM And before this, setting forth examples of the chastity of pagan matrons who preferred chastity to life, he says: *I will touch briefly on the queen of Carthage, who chose rather to burn...*

[On Judith's beauty and virtue] She refused to marry Hierarces the king; and Hasdrubal's wife, seizing her children with both hands, threw herself into the fire below, lest she should suffer the loss of her chastity; and Lucretia, who, having lost the glory of her chastity, was unwilling to survive the stain of a polluted conscience.

[On fasting every day of her life except the Sabbath] Now the Jews fasted from morning until evening, and took no food before the appearance of Hesperus and the stars, just as the Turks also fast to this day.

This was the third endowment of Judith, namely perpetual fasting, which removed the fuel of delicacies—indeed of necessary foods—from the fire of lust. Note the saying: "Withdraw the wood from the hearth, if you wish to quench the flame."

# GEM Hear St. Jerome, Epistle 9, to Salvianus, on preserving widowhood: "But you, who in your husband's tomb have buried all pleasures alike, who washed your face painted with rouge and white lead with tears over his bier, who put on a dark tunic and black shoes, laying aside the bright garment and gilded slipper—you need nothing else but to persevere in fasting. Let pallor and austerity be your jewels; let the softness of feather-beds not pamper youthful limbs; let the heat of baths not inflame the fresh blood of a young woman." Hear what a pagan poet sings from the perspective of a continent widow: "He who first joined me to himself has taken away my love; let him keep it with him and guard it in the tomb." Is it so that the cheapest glass is worth as much as the most precious pearl? If thus a pagan widow, by the common law of nature, condemns all pleasures; what is to be expected of a Christian widow, who owes her chastity not only to him who has died, but also to Him with whom she is to reign? And further: "It is much better for the stomach to ache than for the mind; to rule the body than to serve it; to totter in step than in chastity."

# GEM The same Jerome, Epistle 10, to the widow Furia: "The fiery arrows of the devil," he says, "must be turned back by the rigor of fasts and vigils; for the widow who lives in pleasures is dead while she lives, 1 Tim. 5." And further: "The Apostle chastises his body and subjects it to the rule of the soul, lest he himself fail to keep what he preaches to others; and shall a young woman, with her body inflamed by food, be secure in her chastity? Nor in saying these things do I condemn foods which God created to be used with thanksgiving; but I remove from the young and from girls the incentives to pleasure. Not the fires of Etna, not the volcanic land, not Vesuvius and Olympus burn with such great heat as youthful marrows filled with wine and inflamed with feasting." Then, exhorting her to abstain from wine and flesh, he adds: "Know that vegetables are just as beneficial to Christian young people as that diet." Whence also in another place: "He who is weak," he says, "let him not eat vegetables, and the heat of bodies is to be tempered with cooler foods." And soon: "Whatever makes a seed-bed of pleasures, consider it poison. Sparing food, and a stomach always hungry, is to be preferred to three-day fasts. And it is much better to take a little each day than rarely to take enough. That rain is best which descends gently upon the earth. A sudden and excessive downpour sweeps the fields headlong away. When you eat, remember that you must straightway pray and straightway read." And after some words: "O if you could see your sister! She makes fasting a sport, prayer a delight."

[On her husband leaving her great riches] which she herself did not take for her own use, but distributed for the use of the needy. Admirably says St. Bernard, sermon on the feast of All Saints: "What is more admirable, or what martyrdom more severe, than to hunger amid banquets, to be cold amid many and costly garments, to be oppressed with poverty amid the riches which the world offers, which the Evil One displays, which our own appetite desires? Shall not he rightly be crowned who has so striven—rejecting the world as it promises, mocking the enemy as he tempts, and (what is more glorious) triumphing over himself and crucifying his itching concupiscence?" # GEM

[And she was exceedingly beautiful, because she feared the Lord greatly] This fear was not servile but filial, by which Judith worshipped and revered God as her Lord, indeed as her Father, in the highest degree; and therefore she was diligent to please Him in all things, and took care not to offend Him in any matter, however small. And by this holiness she prepared herself for so great a deed, that she might be worthy to be chosen by God to overthrow the camp of the enemy and to liberate the people.
[Allegorically] Judith was a type of the Blessed Virgin, of whom...

Ye tempt means ye embitter, provoke, and stir to wrath, while ye prescribe laws to Him that unless He succour you within five days ye will hand the city to the enemies, when rather ye ought to invoke Him with humble prayer that He may have mercy on you and Himself appoint for you the day and manner of your deliverance.
NON ENIM QUASI HOMO SIC DEUS COMMINABITUR
column4: The meaning is: let us not fear the threats of Holofernes but those of God; for the threats of Holofernes are proud, empty, and vain, as are those of other men; but God's threats are certain, fixed, and effectual. For God when angered assuredly and inevitably inflicts what He threatens.

column3: ET IDEO HUMILIEMUS ILLI ANIMAS NOSTRAS
column4: Through humble penitence, fasting, prayer, and supplication, that He may pardon our sins and deliver us from Holofernes.

column3: AD ELOQUIUM VESTRUM CORDA EORUM ERIGITE
column4: That is, by your speech raise their hearts to magnanimity and firm confidence in God as their deliverer.

column3: ABRAHAM TENTATUS EST ET PER MULTAS TRIBULATIONES PROBATUS DEI AMICUS EFFECTUS EST
column4: Most notably when commanded by God to sacrifice his only-begotten Isaac he was willing to do so, Gen. 22. See what is said there.

column3: OMNES QUI PLACUERUNT DEO PER MULTAS TRIBULATIONES TRANSIERUNT FIDELES
column4: That is, cleaving faithfully to God and seeking and awaiting deliverance from tribulation from Him. See what is said at James 1:1 and following, and Ecclesiasticus 1:1 and following.

column3: ET NOS ERGO NON ULCISCAMUR NOS
column4: That is, let us not in the manner of those seeking vengeance be indignant and murmur against God, as though wishing to take revenge upon Him for the disasters and terrors He sends upon us.

column3: ET DIXIT ILLIS JUDITH SICUT QUOD POTUI LOQUI DEI ESSE COGNOSCITIS
column4: That is, what I have been able to say ye recognize as suggested and inspired to me by God.

column3: [On Judith prefiguring Mary] The most renowned and most famous Name is with thee; therefore thou also art most renowned and most celebrated: thus Mary is signified by the most famous name of Judith, of whom it is written that there was none who spoke an evil word of her.

That which I have purposed to do (namely, by cunning to kill Holofernes), prove whether it be of God: the word "if" here is not that of one who doubts, but of one who affirms; it is the same as if she said: As you have approved my words as proceeding from God, so also approve my purpose, which I have resolved to carry out with God as the mover—namely, that I should go to Holofernes and slay him. She uses "if," however, as though doubting, for the sake of modesty, because the thing had not yet been done but was to be done; although she herself was certain of the happy outcome from God's prompting. It is as if she said: Approve, that is, see and attend to what I am about to do, and from the outcome prove and judge whether it be of God; as if to say: When you shall see the thing happily accomplished, you will approve it and judge that it was undertaken with God as its author. She speaks in general terms and does not explain in particular what she has in mind, for she knew that if she did so, the matter would not remain secret but would be carried to Holofernes. She also knew that many would condemn her purpose as rash. Hence the Greek text reads: "I will do a thing which shall go down to the children of our nation through all generations."

You shall stand at the gate this night, and I will go out with my handmaid: as if to say, open to me by night the gates closed from fear of the enemy, and allow me to go out with my maidservant. For she asks that the gates be opened to her by night.

With my handmaid: Many think that "abra" is the proper name of Judith's maidservant, but they are mistaken, for it is a common noun meaning a maidservant. Hence in chapter 10, verses 2 and following, where our translator renders it "ancilla," the Greek has "abra." It is said to be as it were "chabra," meaning a companion, or rather as it were "abra," that is, one who passes alongside, a attendant: for "habar" in Hebrew means "he passed." So Pagninus. Moreover, this was not an ordinary maidservant of Judith's, but one who was set over all her possessions, as the Greek text states. Hence the Greeks call honored maidservants (whom the Italians call "Cameriere") "abras," as is evident from Menander in the pseudo-Hercules. So in Genesis 24:61 the maidservants of Rebekah are called "abras," as likewise the maidservant of Pharaoh's daughter in Exodus 2:5. Some add that "abra" properly denotes a maidservant who shares one's table and acts as secretary, and is in charge of the larder; for this is what the word means among the Spartans.
But as for you, I desire that you search not into what I am doing: as being approved and decreed by God. She prudently conceals her secret, for the whole force of this action depended upon secrecy.

And until I bring you word, do nothing else but pray for me to the Lord: # GEM Here learn that in a great and public cause you should seek public and continuous prayers, that God may direct and prosper the matter. The weapons therefore with which Judith cut off Holofernes were the public litanies and prayers of the whole people. Note this passage in support of litanies. Hear St. Augustine, Sermon 228 on the seasons, which is on Judith: "Most holy Judith, by whose prayers heaven was opened, fashioned with the art of prayer victorious weapons with which to fight against adversaries, and as a woman to avenge the trembling men. The city was squalid under the siege of barbarian ferocity. For all were so faint that they judged it more fitting to be handed over into the hands of the enemy than to be harassed by the ravages of deadly famine. Behold, Judith after prayer and sackcloth, after ashes, after the adornments of a maiden returns, the hope of all peoples comes forth, about to restore security to anxious peoples, consoling them with her wisdom; and promising a victory that every nation would marvel at, she requests that the gate be opened to her with the bolts drawn back a little. She went out secure, content with the company of one maiden alone, and hastened with confident steps into the camp of the enemies. Within the walls she was anxious; she becomes secure once she reaches those to be feared." And toward the end: "By the favor of prayer, chastity kept its own modesty intact and undisturbed, and deceived another's, etc. For where heaven is assailed by continual prayers, the power of arms is always trampled underfoot. Prayer preserved the chastity of that glorious woman, yet a multitude could not preserve the enemy. One woman availed to avenge the mourning city, yet so great an army could not guard its king." And in Sermon 229: "Judith, a warlike spirit, besieged an alien breast. She, casting off the timidity of women, seeking victory in dangers, neither feared death in perils, nor dreaded uncertainty."

The occasion for killing him craftily while he was off guard. For even if Holofernes was to be swept away into a shameful and fornicating love for her, Judith nonetheless did not intend this, but merely permitted it as a means to accomplish her deed, by which she destined him for slaughter, which she could not carry out by any other means. She intended therefore to attract Holofernes by her beauty toward an honorable and matrimonial love, so that she might kill him while enamored of her and off his guard. This deed of Judith, for whom God worked in a singular manner by killing Holofernes and liberating Judea, ought rather to be excused and ascribed to love of country and to a special divine instinct, than to be accused of lust or scandal, as Holy Scripture, the Fathers, and the Interpreters excuse it, and especially St. Ambrose in his book On Widows. # GEM

Grant me constancy of soul. Note: Every virtue, but especially constancy, must be sought from God, and this especially by the weak and feeble — such as women, children, the aged, and the sick — when they undertake something heroic surpassing the powers of nature, such as was this work of Judith; and this both because man, made fragile and inconstant by sin, and because God says: "I am who I am," that is, He who is constant by essence, the fountain and author of all being. But note that Judith seeks this clothed in sackcloth and sprinkled with ashes, and prostrate on the ground (verse 1), because prayer, in order to obtain constancy, must be armed with penitence, labor, and mortification of the flesh; for he who is soft and indulges in bodily pleasures cannot be strong and constant. Ashes are a sign of death, because the memory of death greatly arouses constancy, as does the brevity of life and of the labor and pain to be endured therein. Finally, prostration is a sign of humility, by which one, acknowledging his own frailty, implores God's help and obtains constancy from Him.

FOR YOUR STRENGTH DOES NOT CONSIST IN NUMBERS, that is, you do not achieve victory through many and mighty soldiers, but through a single weak and unwarlike woman you will accomplish the whole matter. Thus Artabanus said to Xerxes, exulting in his vast forces: "A great army is put to rout by a small one, as often as God strikes those whom He detests with fear or with a thunderbolt," as Heliodorus reports in book 7.
Presuming, that is, exceedingly confident.

MEMENTO, DOMINE, TESTAMENTI TUI,] that is, of the covenant entered into with the Hebrews through Moses, David, and other princes. UT DOMUS TUA (thy temple) IN SANCTIFICATIONE TUA, that is, may it remain in the holy religion and worship of thee, by which it is truly sanctified.

Factum est autem, cum cessasset clamare ad Dominum: After completing her prayer, Judith rose from the place where she had lain prostrate before the Lord. So great was her confidence that God had heard her prayer that she at once rose up and prepared herself for the journey.
Vocavitque abram suam: She called her handmaid — for "abra" means handmaid, as I have noted — and descended into her house, removed the sackcloth, and put off the garments of her widowhood.

Et lavit corpus suum: She washed her body, anointed herself with the finest myrrh, arranged the hair of her head, placed a mitre upon her head, clothed herself in her garments of gladness, put sandals on her feet, and took bracelets, lilies, earrings, and rings, and adorned herself with all her ornaments.

Cui etiam Dominus contulit splendorem: God added a supernatural brightness to her natural beauty, because all this adornment proceeded not from lust but from virtue. Therefore the Lord increased this beauty in her, so that she appeared to all eyes with incomparable comeliness. # GEM This verse teaches that when a good end sanctifies an action, God himself co-operates with and blesses the means employed, even such as are in themselves indifferent.

On the phrase "non ex libidine, sed ex virtute pendebat": Note that the same external act may be virtuous or sinful according to the intention. Judith adorned herself not to please men or to excite lust, but to accomplish by her beauty what she could not accomplish by force of arms. Hence God rewarded her intention by augmenting the very beauty she employed as an instrument of his glory.

Imposuit itaque abrae suae ascoperam vini: She placed upon her handmaid a leathern bottle of wine, a vessel of oil, parched grain, dried figs, bread, and cheese, for she would eat no food of the Gentiles. These provisions were for her own use in the camp of Holofernes, that she might observe the dietary laws of her people.

Cumque venissent ad portam civitatis: When they came to the gate of the city they found Ozias and the elders of the city waiting there, perhaps keeping watch through the night according to their custom in time of siege.

Qui cum vidissent eam, stupentes mirati sunt nimis pulchritudinem ejus: They were struck with amazement at her beauty, which was now enhanced by the divine splendor that had been added to it. They who had known her before as a widow in sackcloth now scarcely recognised her.

Nihil tamen interrogantes eam, dimiserunt transire: Though astonished, they asked her nothing, restrained by a divine instinct, lest by questioning they should impede what God was bringing to pass. They blessed her: "God of our fathers give thee grace, and strengthen all the counsel of thy heart by his power, that Jerusalem may glory over thee, and thy name be in the number of the holy and just."

Fiat, fiat: All who were present answered with one voice, "So be it, so be it," as with a solemn Amen, approving her purpose though they knew it not fully, and calling down upon her the blessing of God.

Judith vero, orans Dominum, transivit per portas: Judith, praying to the Lord as she went, passed through the gates with her handmaid. She did not cease from prayer even while walking; her heart was continually lifted up to God even when her feet were carrying her toward the enemy camp.

Factum est autem, cum descenderet montem, circa ortum diei, occurruerunt ei exploratores Assyriorum: As she descended the mountain about daybreak the Assyrian scouts encountered her and seized her, asking: "Whence comest thou, or whither dost thou go?" Providence so ordered it that she was taken at dawn, when her beauty could be seen to full effect, and that she fell into the hands of men who would bring her before Holofernes.

Filia sum Hebraeorum, ideo ego fugi a facie eorum: She replied that she was a daughter of the Hebrews who had fled from her people, because she foresaw that they would be given over to spoliation, since by despising the Assyrians and refusing to surrender they would find no mercy. Here Judith speaks with prudent ambiguity: what she says contains no formal lie, for the Hebrews would indeed be given to spoliation if they sinned against God, and she does not say she herself wishes this or that it will certainly come to pass unconditionally.

Vadam ad faciem principis Holofernis: She declared her purpose to go before the prince Holofernes to reveal the secrets of her people and show him by what approach he might subdue them without the loss of a single man from his army. This was a stratagem of war, not a lie in the strict sense, for she intended indeed to appear before Holofernes and to be the instrument of his overthrow, which she truly believed would come without loss to any true servant of God.

Considerabant faciem ejus, et erat in oculis eorum stupor: When the soldiers heard her words they gazed upon her face and were seized with amazement, marvelling greatly at her beauty. Divine grace so wrought upon her appearance that men were disarmed of suspicion and filled with admiration.

Conservasti animam tuam, eo quod tale reperisti consilium: The scouts said she had saved her own life by devising such a plan as to descend to their lord. They meant she had done wisely for herself; God intended she was saving not her own life but the lives of all her people.

Cum steteris in conspectu ejus, bene tibi faciet: They assured her that when she stood in the presence of Holofernes he would treat her well and she would be most welcome in his heart. So they led her to the tent of Holofernes and announced her.

Statim captus est in suis oculis Holofernes: As soon as she came into his presence Holofernes was immediately captivated by his own eyes, that is, by the sight of her. The word "captus" is pregnant with irony: he who came to take captives was himself taken; he who sought to ensnare Israel was ensnared by beauty employed in the service of God.

Quis contemnat populum Hebraeorum, qui tam decoras mulieres habent: His attendants said: "Who can despise the people of the Hebrews who have such beautiful women? It is well worth fighting against them for their sake." These words, meant in a profane sense, contain an unwitting tribute to a people among whom such virtue and beauty could be found together.

Videns itaque Judith Holofernem sedentem in conopeo: Judith saw Holofernes sitting under a canopy woven of purple and gold and emeralds and precious stones — a display of Oriental luxury and pride befitting the general of the greatest empire of the age.

Adoravit eum prosternens se super terram: She prostrated herself and adored him with civil adoration, as was the custom before kings and great lords in the East. This was not the adoration of latria due to God alone, but the reverence of a subject before a ruler, lawful in itself. The servants of Holofernes raised her up at their master's command, and so she stood before him whom she was destined to destroy.

Lapide on "She anointed herself with myrrh" (some wrongly read "with myrtle"): that is, with ointment (for this is what the Greek μύρον means), with the BEST [ointment], so that she might spread a wondrous fragrance from herself, and thereby strike the nostrils of Holofernes, just as her beauty struck his eyes, and her pleasing words his ears. Hear St. Augustine, or rather St. Ambrose — for it bears the style of Ambrose, as the Parisian Doctors noted — Sermon 229 de Tempore, which is on Judith: "The avenger of her people took up the weapons of adornment, not about to undergo marriage from war; but as a warrior she went forth from the city as a bride, nor was the lightness of ornament, which was fitted for the slaying of so great a head, levity." She proceeds, led by the divine spirit, and content with the singular comfort of her handmaid. She preserves the beauty that charms, and lest chastity, carefully guarded, be violated, she addresses Holofernes, so that the desire might be conceived in the morning, namely of matrimonial union, which Judith was conceiving in her mind, and which she could easily have persuaded Holofernes of by her beauty and fair speech, had she wished to strive toward that end.

Lapide on "And she put a mitre upon her head": A mitre is a linen band gleaming with gems and small golden plates and trembling ornaments. For this is what the Hebrew צְנִיף (tsanif) means, as I said at Isaiah 3. The mitre alludes to "mithra," which word in Persian signifies the sun; for the sun is crowned as it were with a mitre by its purest and most brilliant rays. Virgins likewise, and the chaste, such as Judith, are like certain suns crowned with mitres (mithrati), radiating on all sides with the splendour of their chastity.
Lapide on "And lilies": that is, bracelets having the appearance and form of lilies. In Greek it is ψέλια, that is bracelets, arm-bands, namely ornaments of the hands and arms.

# GEM Lapide on "To whom also the Lord gave increased beauty": both by causing her adornments to appear more beautiful and splendid to men, and by truly breathing upon her face a certain celestial light and splendour, such as He breathed upon Moses (Exodus 34:29), and upon St. Anthony, St. Ignatius, and other eminent Saints, upon whom He breathed rays of light so that their holiness might shine forth in their faces. See here a new art of fighting, by which Judith, adorning herself, defended both her chastity and the lives of her fellow citizens; for other virgins are accustomed to protect their chastity by disfiguring themselves. In the year of the Lord 870, when the Danes invading England came to the monastery of Coldingham, Abbess Ebba, in order to protect her own chastity and that of her sisters, cut off her own nose and upper lip, and persuaded her sisters to do the same. Wherefore the invading Danes burned the monastery together with the virgins. Thus Matthew of Westminster reports in his annals for the said year. Surely they followed by instinct the saying and deed of St. Pelagia the virgin, who, constrained by similar necessity lest she suffer the loss of her virginity, said: "Let us die, or if they are unwilling, let us die." God is not offended by the remedy, and faith washes away the deed; and so she cast herself from the roof, as St. Ambrose reports in book 3 On Virgins, and St. Chrysostom in his homily on her praises.

Lapide on "Because all this dressing up was not from wantonness, but from virtue": Because it proceeded from zeal for freeing the fatherland from the destruction and ruin of the tyrant. Hear St. Ambrose in his book On Widows: "And well did she, about to fight, resume the adornments of a married woman, because the memorials of a husband are weapons of chastity. For a widow could neither please others nor conquer in any other way. Why should we pursue the rest — that amid so many thousands of enemies she remained chaste? Why should we proclaim her wisdom — that she devised a plan of this sort, so that, while she lures the powerful man by his intemperance, she might ward off those inferior to herself, prepare an occasion for victory, reserve the merit of abstinence, and preserve the grace of chastity?" # GEM

Lapide on "She gave therefore to her maid (handmaid) an ascopera of wine": that is, a wineskin. For ascopera is a Greek word formed from ἀσκός, that is a skin, a bellows, and πήρα, that is a wallet, a small bag, and signifies a pouch or sack in which travellers store their garments, provisions, and other utensils and carry them with them. Suidas uses this word, and Suetonius in the Life of Nero, saying: "Around the neck of another an ascopera was tied, along with a placard: 'I drank what I could; you earned your skin.'" Others read ἄσκωμα or ἀσκοπύτινη, that is a wineskin.
Lapide on "And polenta": Polenta is barley flour, namely barley soaked, then dried and roasted, and ground by a mill into flour, of which I spoke at 1 Kings 17.

Lapide on "And dried figs": that is, masses of dried figs, concerning which Pliny, book 13, chapter 6. Some wrongly read, by metathesis, lapathias for palathas. Furthermore Judith took this provision with her so that she would not be forced to eat the foods of Holofernes. For the more religious among the Jews so abhorred fellowship with Gentiles that they could not endure even to eat their foods, especially because Gentiles frequently offered their foods to their idols so that they became things sacrificed to idols (idolothyta), concerning which [further discussion follows].

Many think that Judith here and in the following verses is lying, but doing so officiously for the liberation of her people, and that she did not sin, because she believed that in such a case, in such great necessity, lying was lawful, indeed honest and pious, as Plato thought, and indeed Origen, Cassian, and other Fathers, whom I cited on Exodus 1. So Lyra, Abulensis, Mariana, Emmanuel Sa, and St. Thomas, 2.2, q. 110, art. 3, ad 3.

But Rabanus, the Glossa, Hugo, Dionysius, Serarius, Salianus, Sanchez, and St. Augustine in his book On Lying excuse Judith from lying, because God was directing her in this whole work and preserving her from sin. # GEM

Judith speaks truth ambiguously to the Assyrian enemies who are unjustly attacking her, in order to destroy them, yet she does not lie. She says therefore: "I fled," that is, I departed quickly; for "to flee" is often the same as to go away swiftly and to tear oneself from someone, "from their face": or "I fled," that is, I took on the appearance and semblance of one fleeing, or of a deserter, as if like a fugitive by night I fell into your hands and surrender myself to you. So Serarius, Sanchez, Salianus and others.

Because I knew that it would come to pass that they should be given to you for a prey: for this was indeed going to happen in truth, if you consider human reason and foresight; for the Hebrews could not humanly and with human strength resist the Assyrians; but Judith, relying on a higher counsel and oracle, knew this would not happen except through the heavenly providence and correction of God.

I will go before the face of the prince Holofernes, to declare unto him their secrets: namely, that the besieged citizens were suffering extreme thirst and famine, and were therefore murmuring against their princes, urging them to surrender to Holofernes, etc. She speaks truth: yet this was not her ultimate end, but an intermediate one. For she planned by this to bind Holofernes to herself, so that, having bound him and made him secure, she might slay him when the occasion arose. For, as Salianus rightly observes, it must be noted here that in a just war, such as was that of the Jews against the Assyrians, not only open force but also hidden and concealed force is lawful and just; and those whom it is lawful to lead to death may rightly be led into a trap. Deceit and stratagem are by their nature morally indifferent, and receive their malice or goodness from the end to which they are referred. Since therefore Judith's end is good, the deceit itself will also be praiseworthy; and the same must be judged concerning her adornment and bodily decoration.

And she was in his eyes a stately one, because they admired her beauty exceedingly: the entire attention of their minds therefore was seized and absorbed in beholding and admiring her surpassing beauty, so that they could neither see nor think of anything else. Judith knew this, and therefore assumed such great adornment in order to capture their eyes and minds, and having captured them to deceive them, and to slay Holofernes, and thus to scatter his camp. # GEM Hear St. Augustine (or rather St. Ambrose, or certainly Maximus; for many sermons of St. Ambrose are found word for word in St. Maximus Bishop of Turin, since the style of both is very similar), Sermon 228 on the Seasons, which is on Judith: "Of which woman the hostile army greatly feared the novelty of her treacherous beauty, so that in her service they lost their strength, threw down their arms, and bent their necks. By the ranks subjected to her is Holofernes' deceit led to the praetorium, and the victory of the great city." Morally, learn here how dangerous and destructive is the sight of women: examples of which are Samson, David, Solomon, and a thousand others.

AND THEY LED HER TO THE TENT OF HOLOFERNES. The Greek adds: with her maidservant, and a joined chariot and a guard of a hundred men. And shortly after: And he went out (Holofernes) into the proscenium, that is, the vestibule of the curtained chamber, and silver lamps preceded him; both because it was night, namely the golden twilight of dawn, as is clear from verse 11; and because fire was customarily carried before kings and princes. # GEM
Aequo animo esto: Holofernes seeks to calm Judith's fear, saying he has never harmed any man who wished to serve Nabuchodonosor. A flattering opening designed to win her trust.

S. Augustine, serm. 278: "When Holofernes saw her, he was dissolved in his senses, about to lose his soul together with his head. For the disgrace of young men lay captive to a woman's face; it was granted to a woman to disarm young men and to defeat conquerors; to defend an anxious city and to overthrow a barbarian warrior. The sincere woman deceived the corrupt, the chaste deceived the polluted, the modest slew the adulterer, the sober slew the drunkard." # GEM

Augustine continues: "Although that warrior of arms madly attended to the face of that most chaste woman, she nevertheless thought, for the purpose for which she had come, about his head, by the removal of which she would aid the exhausted citizens and protect, defend, and vindicate many heads." # GEM
Populus autem tuus si non contempsisset me: Holofernes excuses his aggression against Israel by blaming their contempt of him, claiming he would not have raised his spear against them otherwise.
Qua ex causa recessisti ab illis: Holofernes asks Judith directly why she has left her people and come to him, seeking to understand her motive and test her sincerity.

Sume verba ancillae tuae: Judith begins her response with studied humility, calling herself his handmaid, and promises that if he follows her words the Lord will bring the matter to perfect completion through him.

Vivit enim Nabuchodonosor rex terrae: Judith swears by the life of Nabuchodonosor and by the virtue that is in Holofernes for the correction of all erring souls, flattering him extravagantly. She speaks truth in false guise, for God would indeed use him as an instrument of correction.

Note that Judith attributes to Nabuchodonosor a quasi-divine authority over beasts as well as men, words that are flattery directed at Holofernes but contain an ironic truth: God does use even pagan kings as instruments of His providence.

Nuntiatur enim animi tui industria: Judith praises Holofernes as universally renowned for prudence and power, the sole good and mighty man in all Nabuchodonosor's kingdom. This is deliberate flattery to lower his guard.

Nec hoc latet quod locutus est Achior: Judith shows she knows what Achior said in the council, and what Holofernes commanded to be done with him, thus displaying her knowledge and winning credibility.

Constat enim Deum nostrum sic peccatis offensum: Judith reveals the true theological reason: God has been so offended by Israel's sins that He has commanded through the prophets that the people be delivered up on account of their sins. This is the key to her stratagem.

Tremor tuus super ipsos est: Because the children of Israel know they have offended their God, the fear of Holofernes has fallen upon them. Judith uses their guilty conscience as an argument for their imminent defeat.

Insuper etiam fames invasit eos: She adds that famine has struck them, and for lack of water they are already reckoned among the dead. The physical desperation of the besieged is offered as further proof that God has abandoned them.
Denique hoc ordinant ut interficiant pecora sua: In their extremity the people plan to slaughter their own animals and drink their blood, a thing forbidden by the Law.

Et Sancta Domini Dei sui quae praecepit Deus non contingi: They intend to consume the first-fruits of grain, wine, and oil consecrated to God, which the Law strictly forbade them to touch. Judith presents this imminent sacrilege as the sin that will seal their doom.

Statim captus est in suis oculis Holofernes: Holofernes was immediately captivated by Judith in his own eyes. Augustine: "He lay, the disgrace of young men, a captive to a woman's face; it was permitted to a woman to disarm young men and defeat conquerors." # GEM

Sedentem in conopeo: The conopeum is a curtain tapering to a sharp cone at the top and spread in a circle below to keep off gnats; κώνωψ means gnat. Such curtains were commonly placed around camp-beds and portable couches. The Greek states that Holofernes sat upon his bed within the conopeum. Italians and French similarly name such curtain-hangings after this function.

Greek addition to verse 18: "Because it is not good that one woman remain alive who could deceive the whole earth through women as beautiful as Judith." Lapide notes: they speak truth unwittingly, for they themselves were deceived and slaughtered by the Hebrews through Judith.

THEN HOLOFERNES SAID TO HER: BE OF GOOD COURAGE.] Holofernes observed that Judith was fearful and trembling, both because she was a woman accustomed to solitude standing among so many thousands of armed men and enemies, and because the consciousness of so great a deed was beating upon her mind with fear, lest perhaps it might not succeed, but the matter be discovered, and she herself incur danger of her life. For granted that, God animating her, her spirit was undaunted; yet rarely was the inferior part of her mind unaffected by weakness. What wonder? since Christ, the most courageous, about to enter upon his Passion, said of himself: The spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak; Matt. 26.
NOR HAVE I EVER HURT A MAN,] much less a woman.

[On the Jews resolving to consume the firstfruits, tithes, and holy oil] These things they devised to spend, and wished to consume what they ought not to touch with their hands: therefore, because they do these things, it is certain that they shall be given over to destruction.
Which I thy handmaid knowing, fled from them, and the Lord hath sent me to tell thee these very things.
For I thy handmaid worship God even now with thee: and thy handmaid will go out and pray to God,

and he will tell me when he will render them their sin, and I will come and tell thee, so that I will bring thee through the midst of Jerusalem, and thou shalt have all the people of Israel as sheep without a shepherd, and not so much as one dog shall bark against thee:
because these things are told me by the providence of God.
And because God is angry with them, I am sent to tell thee these very things.
And all these words pleased Holofernes, and his servants, and they admired her wisdom, and they said one to another:
There is not such another woman upon earth in look, in beauty, and in sense of words.
And Holofernes said to her: God hath done well who sent thee before the people, that thou mightest give them into our hands:

and because thy promise is good, if thy God shall do this for me, he shall also be my God, and thou shalt be great in the house of Nabuchodonosor, and thy name shall be renowned through all the earth.

He shall make a perfect end with thee — that is, God will give a perfect victory, not to thee, as thou thinkest, O Holofernes, but to me and to the Jews over thee. This is a stratagem of war, by which Judith plays upon Holofernes with an ambiguity of words and so leads him into the snare and trap of death. For, as the Poet says: "Who in an enemy cares whether it be craft or valor?" Thus the Delphic Apollo mocked Pyrrhus when he consulted him concerning victory over the Romans, saying: "I say that thou, son of Aeacus, canst conquer the Romans" — which Pyrrhus wrongly applied to himself, as though he were to conquer the Romans, whereas Apollo meant that the Romans would conquer Pyrrhus, as indeed they did in the event.

For Nabuchodonosor the King of the earth liveth, and his virtue (his strength, his fortitude) liveth. Judith swears by the life and virtue of Nabuchodonosor, just as Joseph swears by the health of Pharaoh, Gen. 42:15.
But even the beasts of the field (namely horses, camels, oxen, asses, sheep, etc.) obey him.
It hath been declared to every age — that is, to the whole present world. So the Greek.

That thou only art good — that is, benign and beneficent, according to that word of Christ, Luke 22:25: "The kings of the Gentiles lord it over them, and they that have power over them are called Benefactors." Hence the title of governors was "most excellent," as: most excellent Felix, most excellent Festus. See what is said at Acts 24:3. The sense therefore is: Thou alone art the true and great prince in the kingdom of Nabuchodonosor, as being equally benign and liberal as he is powerful, and therefore Nabuchodonosor hath committed all his affairs to thee. Some explain "good" as meaning "strong."

As he commanded by his prophets. For God sent Elijah, Elisha, Isaiah, Hosea, Jeremiah, and the other prophets, who rebuked the idols and crimes of Israel and threatened and foretold destruction upon them by the Assyrians, Chaldeans, and other enemies.
Famine hath seized them — because where there is thirst, there is also hunger; for food cannot be moistened and eaten by one who thirsts without drink.

And that they should drink their blood — in place of water, which they lack to quench their extreme thirst; moreover the eating and drinking of blood was forbidden to the Jews by God under pain of death, as is clear from Lev. 17.
And the holy things of the Lord — those things which are consecrated to God, that is, dedicated and set apart — "these they have thought to spend" — to relieve their extreme thirst and hunger.

Therefore because they do these things, it is certain that they shall be given to destruction. It is certain, if thou lookest to natural causes and the ordinary course of affairs: for famine and thirst will drive them to ruin. Their sins will do the same, by which they have merited the wrath and vengeance of God — unless some holy person should mitigate the wrath of God, as I hope to mitigate it by my prayers.

The Lord hath sent me to tell thee these very things. For God sent Judith to Holofernes, so that by reporting these things to him she might win his favor, and having obtained it, might kill him incautiously by a holy stratagem.

And I will pray to God, and he will tell me when he will render to them their sin, and I will come and tell thee, so that I will bring thee through the midst of Jerusalem, and thou shalt have all the people of Israel as sheep that have no shepherd, and not so much as one dog shall bark against thee. # GEM Many think Judith cannot here be excused from falsehood. But I excuse her in two ways and on two grounds. The first is that she speaks according to natural causes and the common course of nature, by which the Jews, reduced to extremity and placed under the offense and wrath of God, necessarily had to perish unless God should aid them by some supernatural or extraordinary means. Thus Elisha, when Hazael inquired about the recovery of king Ben-hadad who was sick, answered that he would be healed, yet would die — because, that is, inasmuch as the disease itself was concerned, it was not mortal, wherefore from it he would recover, yet he would die because he would be smothered by Hazael, 2 Kgs. 8:10.

So here Judith asserts that the Hebrews would be destroyed by Holofernes on account of their sins, because by human strength they could not escape his hand; yet they were to escape through the prayers of Judith and the priests, who sought and obtained heavenly aid from God.

The sense therefore is: The Hebrews, if thou lookest to natural causes, are plainly to be destroyed and subjugated by thee on account of their sins. Wherefore I will pray to God about this matter, and if indeed He wills utterly to destroy them, and indicates this to me as is His custom, and wills that I declare the same to thee — as it seems likely to come about if thou lookest to the common course of God's providence — I will announce this very thing to thee according to the mind of God, so that I will lead thee through the midst of Jerusalem and subject the whole people to thee. For if God so wills and ordains, I must wholly obey Him even to the harm and destruction of my people; for if He wishes to use me as a rod to chastise them, I ought and will serve Him as a rod in this chastisement.

For in a similar manner God chastised Rehoboam and the Jews by creating through the prophet Ahijah king Jeroboam and delivering to him the kingdom of the ten tribes of Israel. So God through Isaiah ch. 45 summoned Cyrus to chastise the crimes of many nations. So through Jeremiah He summoned Nabuchodonosor and the Chaldeans to the destruction of Jerusalem, which Jeremiah therefore frequently and persistently threatened against the Jews, and even urged them to surrender to Nabuchodonosor besieging them, for they could not escape his hand, since the just will of God and the punishment of their crimes stood firm. Hence Nabuchodonosor, learning of these oracles of Jeremiah concerning himself, besieged Jerusalem for eighteen months until he captured it; and when it was taken, he ordered that Jeremiah be treated kindly and set free on account of these his oracles concerning him, as is clear from Jer. 39:11. Indeed Abulensis on 2 Kgs. 24, q. 7, and on Isaiah ch. 5, v. 25 and ch. 10:5, thinks it probable that Nabuchodonosor, Shalmaneser, and similar rulers subjugated the Jews by revelation and command of God; on which matter I have said more in the Proem on Isaiah, chs. 36 and 37.

# GEM So St. Lupus, when Attila king of the Huns was besieging Troyes (of which city he himself was bishop), asked him what he was and what he wished; and hearing him reply: "I am Attila, king of the Huns, the scourge of God," he said: "Come then, scourge of my God, and use thyself as God grants thee" — and so led him through the midst of the city, but as though with eyes and mind struck blind, so that he harmed none of the inhabitants, as Baronius records from the Life of St. Lupus and other sources, vol. 6, anno Christi 451. The same therefore Judith could have done, and indeed would have been bound to do, with regard to Holofernes, had God so commanded her. But Judith knew that God would not command this of her, but would do the contrary, so that through this simulation she might betray Holofernes. This, however, she did not wish, nor was she obliged, to reveal to him.
The second way of excusing Judith from lying is by saying that she used ambiguous words, which Holofernes applied to himself, whereas Judith was applying them to herself and to the Jews.

She says therefore: I WILL PRAY TO GOD, AND HE WILL TELL ME WHEN HE WILL REPAY THEM FOR THEIR SIN — if, that is, He has decreed to punish them according to their merits, as it appears. But I know from God's revelation that at this time He will not punish them; yet this must now be kept silent by me, nor can I disclose it to you — nevertheless, coming soon I will announce to you the will and decree of God concerning the deferral of the punishment of the sin of the Jews, and the hastening of the punishment of your tyranny in unjustly attacking the Jews.

For this I will announce to you not in word but in deed, when I inflict a mortal wound upon you, says St. Augustine; AND I WILL LEAD YOU — not conquering and triumphing as you suppose, but conquered, slain, and triumphed over — when I bring your severed head to Bethulia, and thence lead it in triumph through the midst of Jerusalem and throughout the rest of Judea, to the common rejoicing of the Jews. # GEM

AND YOU WILL HAVE ALL THE PEOPLE OF ISRAEL LIKE SHEEP THAT HAVE NO SHEPHERD — for when you have been slain, the people of Bethulia will break forth without any order, without shepherd or leader, to pursue the Assyrians and plunder their camp. Therefore NOT EVEN ONE DOG WILL BARK AGAINST YOU, because dogs do not bark at the dead — which you will be — but at the living.
Thus the words of Judith, taken partly ironically, partly amphibologically, and partly prophetically, are free from falsehood and bear both senses. So Serarius, Sanchez, Salianus, and others.

So Agathocles, besieging Syracuse and threatening those besieged that he would dine in the city the next day, was a true prophet, though against his own meaning; for when the citizens made a sortie and he was brought into the city, he dined there not as victor but as vanquished and captive. This is what St. Augustine says, Sermon 229 de Tempore, which is on Judith: It was not fitting for a chaste woman to lie; she spoke to those who did not understand, and accomplished in deeds what she promised. # GEM

BECAUSE THESE THINGS WERE TOLD TO ME BY THE PROVIDENCE OF GOD. These things therefore guided and directed Judith, that she might say and do all that she said and did. Therefore she did not lie, for God cannot lie.

THERE IS NOT SUCH A WOMAN UPON EARTH. Hear St. Augustine in the sermon already cited: While the warrior flatters the king with speech of another kind and with figurative words, his mind is dissolved in desire by love of her charms, the countless forces of soldiers grow torpid in royal luxury, the pomp of a wedding is arranged with no one actually marrying, all are undone by drunkenness. It was not a delicate matron who fought, but the sleep which she prepared for you that you might be struck. # GEM

IF YOUR GOD DOES THIS FOR ME (that He subject Jerusalem and the Jews to me), HE WILL BE MY GOD ALSO — that is to say, I will become a proselyte and a Jew, and will worship the true God of Israel. Behold how powerful and effective was the oration of Judith before Holofernes.
Holofernes commanded her to be brought in where his treasures were stored, and ordered her to remain there, and appointed what should be given her from his own table.
Judith answered him and said: I cannot eat of these things which thou commandest to be given me, lest an offence come upon me; but I will eat of the things which I have brought with me.

[On "lest an offence come upon me"] In Greek: skandalon — as if to say: lest, if I eat foods forbidden to Jews among the Gentiles, I offend God; and so bring upon myself punishment and a stumbling-block, that is, a trap and impediment, so that God may not through me accomplish what He has decreed to do for the common good.
Holofernes said to her: If these things which thou hast brought with thee fail thee, what shall we do for thee?

Judith said: As thy soul liveth, my lord, thy handmaid shall not spend all these things till God do by my hand that which I have purposed. And his servants brought her into the tent which he had commanded.
And she requested that she might have liberty to go out at night and before light, to pray and to make supplication to the Lord.

Judith requested this: first, that by prayer and fasting she might prepare and arm herself for the heroic courage required to undertake the great deed she had resolved upon in her mind; second, that she might draw from God the spirit, the manner, and the means of executing it; and finally, that after the deed was accomplished she might freely be allowed to return to Bethulia, since the guards would suppose she was going out in her customary manner to pray.
And he commanded his chamberlains, that she might go out and in to adore her God as it pleased her, for three days.
And she went out in the nights into the valley of Bethulia, and washed herself in a fountain of water.

[On "washed herself in the fountain of water"] It was the custom of the Jews before prayer to wash their hands, feet, and sometimes the whole body — both so that they might approach God with a pure body and soul (for bodily purity reminded them of purity of soul), and so that if they had touched anything unclean and contracted legal irregularity, they might purify themselves by washing. This same practice was observed by early Christians, as I noted at 1 Tim. 2:8 on the words "lifting up pure hands." # GEM Moreover, this fountain near Bethulia still exists and is visited by pilgrims, as Adrichomius testifies in his account of Bethulia.
And when she went up, she prayed to the Lord God of Israel to direct her way to the deliverance of her people.
And going in, she remained pure in the tent, until she took her food in the evening.

Judith therefore fasted from morning until evening; in the evening she took food, and after it she went out at night to prayer, spending the night in it. With these weapons she armed herself for her heroic work. # GEM Hear St. Ambrose, in his book on widows: "Why speak of sobriety? For temperance is the virtue of women. While men were drunk with wine and buried in sleep, a widow took away the sword, put forth her hand, cut off the head of the warrior, and passed undefiled through the midst of the enemy's ranks. Do you see therefore how much drunkenness can harm women, when wine so undoes men that they are conquered by women? Let the widow therefore be temperate; chaste first from wine, that she may be chaste from the adulterer: he will not tempt thee at all, if wine does not tempt thee. For if Judith had drunk, she would have slept with the adulterer. But because she did not drink, her single sobriety could without difficulty both overcome and elude drunken armies."
And it came to pass on the fourth day, that Holofernes made a supper for his servants, and said to Vagao his eunuch: Go, and persuade that Hebrew woman, to consent of her own accord to dwell with me.

[On "Vagao his eunuch"] Vagao or Bagao is not so much a proper name, as Serarius holds, as a common Persian word signifying a eunuch. Hence Pagninus in his Hebrew names says that Vagao means "inner" or "innermost," that is, a eunuch or chamberlain; for ghava in Chaldean means interior or intestine. Or Vagao is the same as "raised" or "elevated," from the root gaa, meaning to elevate. For eunuchs held first rank at court among kings, indeed they were their chamberlains and confidants of their secrets, and ministers of procurement for their mistresses; for pandering to their lusts they would bring them beautiful women, as Vagao does here. Pliny confirms this, book 13, chapter 11, where he notes that eunuchs among the Persians were called Bagoi. And Ovid, Amores book 2, Elegy 3: "Bagoas, in whose charge is the keeping of thy mistress, while I go through with thee a few but fitting words, give ear."
For it is a disgrace among the Assyrians, if a woman mock a man, by doing so as to pass free from him.
Then Vagao went in to Judith, and said: Let not this good maid be afraid to go in to my lord, that she may be honoured before his face, and eat and drink wine with joyfulness.
And Judith answered him: Who am I, to gainsay my lord?
All that shall be good and best before his eyes, I will do. And whatsoever shall please him, that shall be best to me all the days of my life.
And she arose and decked herself with her garments, and going in she stood before his face.
And the heart of Holofernes was smitten: for he was burning with the desire of her.
And Holofernes said to her: Drink now, and sit down in joyfulness, for thou hast found favour before me.
And Judith said: I will drink, my lord, because my life is magnified this day above all my days.
And she took and ate and drank before him what her maid had prepared for her.
And Holofernes was made merry on her account, and drank exceeding much wine, so much as he had never drunk in his life.

Bagoas was a eunuch of King Ahasuerus, superintendent of the harem. So likewise, as Quintus Curtius records in book 10, the most wicked eunuch of Alexander the Great was Bagoas, who by false accusations delivered Orsines, satrap of the Persians, to be killed by Alexander. There was also a Bagoas who was a eunuch of Herod. Moreover, Severus Sulpicius errs when he refers the history of Judith to the times of Artaxerxes Ochus, on the grounds that Ochus had a eunuch named Bagoas.

FOR IT IS SHAMEFUL AMONG THE ASSYRIANS IF A WOMAN MOCK A MAN BY PASSING BY HIM UNVIOLATED. See here the utterly corrupt and most impure customs of the Assyrians and Persians, among whom lust was so honorable that it ranged over all women, so that among them it was considered shameful to leave any woman untouched. O the times! Hear Ammianus Marcellinus, book 23, concerning the Persians: "Most of them," he says, "were accustomed to give themselves over rather extravagantly to Venus, and scarcely content with a multitude of concubines, each according to his means took many or few wives, innocent of boyish defilements." Whence among them, with affection dispersed through various lusts, it grows torpid. Strabo likewise reports the same in book 15 near the end, and Cicero in the fifth action against Verres. Hear crimes still more foul. Theodoret, question 14 on Leviticus, says: "The Persians, even to this day, mingle not only with sisters but also with mothers and daughters under the law of marriage." # GEM The same concerning the Medes, Lydians, and Ethiopians is reported by St. Jerome, book 2 against Jovinian. Hence Euripides in his Andromeda says: "Such is the entire race of barbarians."
When evening had come, his servants hurried to their lodgings, and Vagao shut the doors of the bedchamber and went away.
All were weary with wine.
And Judith was alone in the bedchamber.
Now Holofernes lay upon his bed, overcome with excessive drunkenness.
And Judith told her maidservant to stand outside before the bedchamber and to keep watch.
And Judith stood before the bed, praying with tears and the silent movement of her lips,

saying: Strengthen me, O Lord God of Israel, and look in this hour upon the works of my hands, that, as Thou hast promised, Thou mayest raise up Jerusalem Thy city; and that I may bring to completion this thing which, believing it could be done through Thee, I have devised.
And when she had said these things, she approached the column that was at the head of his bed, and loosed his dagger which hung bound upon it.
And when she had drawn it, she seized him by the hair of his head and said: Strengthen me, O Lord God, in this hour.
And she struck him twice upon his neck and cut off his head, and took away his canopy from the columns, and rolled away his headless body.
And after a little while she went out, and delivered the head of Holofernes to her maidservant, and bade her put it in her bag.
And the two went out together, according to their custom, as if for prayer, and passed through the camp, and circling the valley, came to the gate of the city.
And Judith called from afar to the watchmen on the walls: Open the gates, for God is with us, who hath wrought mighty deeds in Israel.
And it came to pass that when the men heard her voice, they called the elders of the city.
And all ran together to her, from the least to the greatest, for they had hoped that she would not return.
And lighting torches they all gathered round her; and she, going up to a higher place, commanded silence. And when all were silent,
Judith said: Praise the Lord our God, who hath not forsaken those that hope in Him;
and by me His handmaid He hath fulfilled His mercy which He promised to the house of Israel; and this night He hath slain by my hand the enemy of His people.

And bringing forth from her bag the head of Holofernes, she showed it to them, saying: Behold the head of Holofernes, the general of the army of the Assyrians, and behold his canopy, wherein he lay in his drunkenness, where the Lord our God struck him by the hand of a woman.

But the Lord Himself liveth, for His angel hath kept me both going hence and abiding there and returning from thence hither; and the Lord hath not suffered me His handmaid to be defiled, but hath brought me back to you without pollution of sin, rejoicing in His victory, in my escape, and in your deliverance.
Give thanks to Him, all of you, for He is good, for His mercy endureth for ever.
And all the people adoring the Lord said to her: The Lord hath blessed thee by His power, because through thee He hath brought our enemies to nought.
And Ozias the prince of the people of Israel said to her: Blessed art thou, daughter, by the Lord the Most High God, above all women upon the earth.
Blessed be the Lord who made heaven and earth, who hath directed thee to the cutting off of the head of the prince of our enemies;

because He hath so magnified thy name this day that thy praise shall not depart from the mouth of men who shall be mindful of the power of the Lord for ever, for whom thou didst not spare thy life by reason of the distress and tribulation of thy people, but didst prevent our ruin in the sight of our God.
And all the people said: So be it, so be it.

And Achior being called came, and Judith said to him: The God of Israel, to whom thou didst bear witness that He taketh vengeance on His enemies, He hath cut off the head of all the unbelievers this night by my hand.

And that thou mayest prove that it is so, behold the head of Holofernes, who in the contempt of his pride despised the God of Israel, and threatened thee with death, saying: When the people of Israel are taken captive...

# GEM S. Ambrose says of the Virgin: "Such was her grace that not only did she preserve the grace of virginity in herself, but she also conferred the mark of integrity upon those whom she visited. She visited John the Baptist, and not without reason did he remain whole in body—he whom the Mother of the Lord exercised for three months with a certain oil of her presence and the ointment of integrity." So says S. Ambrose, and S. Thomas in Book 3 of the Sentences, dist. 3, q. 2, adds: "The beauty of the Blessed Virgin aroused those who gazed upon her to chastity." The same is taught by the Gloss in the sermon on the Conception of the Blessed Virgin, which indeed was granted by a special gift of God.
As the Assyrians feared and mourned Judith, so all demons and the wicked fear the Blessed Virgin. Hence St. Bernard, cited by St. Bonaventure...

Thus, he says, "visible enemies fear [the name of Mary] as though [facing] the dense battle-line of all others, just as the aerial powers [fear] the name of Mary, her patronage and example: they melt and perish like wax before the face of fire, wherever they encounter frequent remembrance of this name, devout invocation [of it], and diligent imitation [of her]."

Judith, shut in her oratory, reconciled God to Israel by her fasts, hair-shirts, prayers, and tears; the Blessed Virgin, shut in her chamber, and imploring God with fasts and unutterable groanings for the reconciliation of the world, merited to receive the Angel of peace as her paranymph [bridal attendant], who would announce to her that she was the mediatrix of this peace through the Incarnation of the Word in her. # GEM

To Judith the victress the people cried out: "Thou art the glory of Jerusalem, thou art the joy of Israel, thou art the honour of our people; because thou hast done manfully, and thy heart has been strengthened." The same and far greater things the Fathers, Doctors, and all the Orthodox sing to the Blessed Virgin.
And he took away the canopy [conopeum], together with the head, so that from it those who had known Holofernes might recognize the head [caput].
He notes that the canopy was woven with gems, so that it might serve both as a trophy of so great a victory and as a proof that the tent was that of Holofernes himself, not of some guard or slave.

AND SHE ROLLED THE BODY FROM THE TRUNK. In Greek: and she rolled his body off the bed, namely onto the floor. Whence afterward Vagao coming found the body lying on the ground, bloody and gore-soaked in its own blood. Judith did this so that when the Hebrews burst out of the city and the Assyrians fled to Holofernes, seeing his headless trunk on the ground, they would be struck dumb with terror and freeze, not daring to take up arms or resist the Hebrews.

AND AFTER A LITTLE WHILE SHE WENT OUT. Because she sat for a short time to recover her breath: for she had expended all her strength and spirit in cutting through the thick neck of Holofernes; moreover the fear of danger, lest the deed be discovered, had cut off her breath, so that she needed to collect herself by sitting and breathing. She did this also to wipe away any blood that had clung to her garment; and especially to give the highest thanks immediately to God for the enemy so happily slain.

BECAUSE THEY FEARED (that is, feared) THAT SHE WOULD NOT COME BACK — thinking her detained as a captive by the Assyrians. Aroused therefore by the unusual news of Judith's return, all ran together; both to see her of whom they had despaired, and to hear from her what she had done and what hope shone upon the besieged who had been reduced to extremity. (He notes the poet's line: "Fear the gods, mindful of right and wrong" — where "sperare" means "to fear.")

AS THE LORD HIMSELF LIVES (that is, I swear by the life of God), BECAUSE HIS ANGEL HAS KEPT ME BOTH GOING HENCE AND ABIDING THERE AND RETURNING FROM THENCE HITHER, AND THE LORD HAS NOT SUFFERED ME HIS HANDMAID TO BE DEFILED. Judith swears that she was guarded by an Angel from all the wantonness and lust of the Assyrians; because this seemed incredible to the Hebrews, that so beautiful a woman should escape the hands of such shameless men. And certainly by human means it was impossible; but to God and his Angels it was possible and easy.
