Judith knew that a guardian Angel had been present with her, either because she had seen him in visible form, or because she had continually heard his inspirations, by which she felt herself warned and directed in all things, so that she knew what she must do and say at each hour and place, or because the Angel gave her other signs of his presence and direction, that she might boldly undertake so heroic a deed and not doubt its happy outcome. # GEM Thus an Angel visibly appeared and directed St. Cecilia with St. Valerian her spouse unto chastity and martyrdom; and himself guarded St. Julian, and Basilia, St. Francesca Romana, and similar eminent saints, especially virgins.

Moreover Serarius, Salianus and others hold that the Angel here was one of the first and highest, on account of the greatness of the business, on which the salvation of the whole people and the Church of God turned. Hence some think he was St. Michael, who was of old the protector of the Synagogue as he now is of the Church. Others think it was St. Gabriel, who as he takes his name from strength (for Gabriel in Hebrew means the strength of God), so he is present and presides over the generous and strong deeds of heroes and heroines (such as this of Judith), as is plain from Daniel 10:20, where he says: "Now I will return to fight against the prince of Persia" (on behalf of the Hebrews). Hence Gabriel was the messenger of the Incarnation of the Word (which was the greatest work of divine power and strength), and therefore the guardian of the Blessed Virgin Mother of God. # GEM Hear blessed Peter Damian, Sermon 1 on the Nativity of the Blessed Virgin, where taking the throne of Solomon flanked by two lions as a mystical figure of the Blessed Virgin: "The two lions," he says, "are Gabriel the Archangel and John the Evangelist, of whom one is appointed guardian of the Virgin's right hand, the other of her left. For Gabriel guarded her mind, John her body, with watchful solicitude." Likewise a lioness was this Judith rending Holofernes: therefore a lion was born to her in Gabriel, who most powerfully both protected her chastity and strengthened her right hand in the slaying of the tyrant.

Others assign this glory of Judith's victory to holy Raphael. For he is the presider over holiness and guide of roads and travelers, as well as guardian of chastity, as I noted in Tobias. He therefore may have led and brought back Judith, chaste, healthy, and holy. Moreover that Angels are present and preside over holy and chaste persons destined for heroic deeds is plain from what I annotated at Exodus 23:20 and following, and Daniel 10:20-21.

LET ALL GIVE PRAISE TO HIM. See here the modesty, gratitude, constancy, and piety of Judith, who ascribes all the glory of her courage and victory not to herself but to God. Moreover the Hebrews were accustomed, upon receiving some signal benefit, to sing in thanksgiving Psalm 117: "Give thanks to the Lord, for he is good, for his mercy endures forever"; as we sing: Te Deum laudamus.

NOW OZIAS THE PRINCE OF THE PEOPLE OF ISRAEL SAID TO HER: BLESSED ARE YOU, DAUGHTER, BY THE LORD THE MOST HIGH GOD, ABOVE ALL WOMEN UPON EARTH. To this the Archangel Gabriel alluded in greeting the Blessed Virgin: "Hail, full of grace, blessed are you among women." For Judith was the shadow and type of the Blessed Virgin; whence there follows:

BECAUSE TODAY YOUR NAME HAS SO SANCTIFIED YOU THAT YOUR PRAISE (in Greek: your hope, that is the memory and praise of your hope, by which hoping in God you brought so great a victory to Israel) SHALL NOT DEPART FROM THE MOUTH OF MEN. Exactly such is the name of the Blessed Virgin.

Judith said to all the people: Hear me, brethren, hang this head upon our walls. The name Maria/Mary is discussed by Blessed Peter Chrysologus, Sermon 146: "This name," he says, "is prophetically genuine, this name is salvific for the reborn, this is the mark of virtue, this the glory of modesty, this the token of chastity, this the sacrifice of God, this the virtue of hospitality, this the fellowship of holiness: rightly therefore is this the maternal name of the mother of Christ." Thus Chrysologus. Methodius in his oration on the Hypapante says: "Your name, O Mother of God, is filled on every side with divine blessings and graces." And St. Bernard, Homily 2 on the Missus Est: "If winds of temptation arise, if you run upon the rocks of tribulation, look to the star, call upon Mary; if anger, or avarice, or the allurement of the flesh has shaken the vessel of your mind, look to Mary; if troubled by the enormity of your crimes, confounded by the foulness of your conscience, terrified by the terror of judgment, you begin to be troubled, or swallowed up, or saddened in the abyss of despair: in dangers, in straits, in doubtful matters, think of Mary, invoke Mary, let her not depart from your lips, let her not depart from your heart." So Serarius, Sanchez, and others. # GEM

For which things (that is, "because," a Hebraism) YOU DID NOT SPARE YOUR SOUL (that is, your life), offering and exposing it to evident peril of death, ON ACCOUNT OF THE DISTRESS AND TRIBULATION OF YOUR RACE (that is, your nation, namely to free your homeland and fellow citizens from siege and destruction), BUT YOU PREVENTED RUIN, forestalling and averting it, lest the city and commonwealth of Israel should fall.
And it shall be, when the sun rises, let every man take his arms and go forth with a rush, not as going down below, but as if making an assault.
Then the scouts will necessarily flee to their prince to rouse him to battle.
And when their commanders shall have run to the tent of Holofernes and found him headless, wallowing in his own blood, fear will fall upon them.
And when you know that they are fleeing, go after them securely, for the Lord will crush them under your feet.

Now when Achior saw the head of Holofernes, being seized with terror (at the horror of the severed head of Holofernes, bloody and gore-covered on all sides), HE FELL UPON HIS FACE TO THE GROUND, AND HIS SOUL FAINTED. In Greek: his spirit failed or was dissolved, that is, he suffered a swooning of the soul, struck with stupor at so unexpected and unanticipated a thing, as various floods of emotion poured together into his heart. For the fear of death impending over him and expressly threatened by name was departing, confidence succeeded; sorrow fled, joy entered; distrust gave way to wonder, and simultaneously with the contemplation of this prodigy, faith, hope, charity and the gifts of the Holy Spirit were expelling unbelief, as will shortly be told. So Salianus.
JUDITH SAID: HANG THE HEAD UPON THE WALLS, so that the Assyrians, seeing their commander slain, might be struck with terror and flee.

AND IT SHALL BE, WHEN THE SUN RISES, LET EVERY MAN TAKE HIS WEAPONS, AND GO FORTH WITH GREAT NOISE, NOT AS THOUGH YOU WOULD GO DOWN BELOW (to a true and serious battle with the far more powerful Assyrians), BUT AS MAKING AN ASSAULT — that is, as though making an attack upon the nearest enemy post. As if to say: Do not engage in full battle array, being so few against countless numbers, but simulate the desire to fight, so that the Assyrians may run to Holofernes to draw up their battle line, and seeing him headless, may be struck with dismay and flee. For Judith did not wish the Assyrians to be attacked before the death of Holofernes had become widely known and had left them bereft of spirit and counsel; but once that was accomplished, she commanded that they immediately rush upon the terrified and fleeing enemy. For if they had given the Assyrians time, those men might by common counsel have chosen another commander who would continue the siege of Bethulia, and so taken the city exhausted by hunger and thirst. But Judith, wisely foreseeing this, commanded that once the death of Holofernes was spread abroad, they should immediately fall upon the stricken foe with all their strength and forces. This done, the stunned enemies were routed and slaughtered as they fled by the Hebrews.

# GEM See here therefore the prudence and military skill of Judith, who pursued the victory she had begun right through to its conclusion; wherefore she was wiser than Hannibal, who, having utterly destroyed the Romans in the battle of Cannae, and when Maharbal the cavalry prefect urged him to march immediately on Rome — for without doubt he would take that stunned city and dine on the Capitol within five days — while Hannibal delayed and said the matter must be considered more carefully, Maharbal rejoined: "You know how to win a victory, Hannibal, but you do not know how to use one." So Livy, book 22. Hear Saint Ambrose in his book On Widows: "Nor is this the work of the right hand alone, but rather a trophy of greater wisdom. For by her hand she conquered Holofernes alone, but by her counsel she conquered the entire army of the enemy. For by hanging up the head of Holofernes — a thing that could not have been devised by the counsel of men — she raised the spirits of her own people and broke those of the enemy: arousing her own with a sense of shame, and striking the foes also with terror, so that they were slain and put to flight. Thus the temperance and sobriety of one widow not only overcame her own nature, but, what is greater, made even the men more valiant."

In like manner David, having cut off the head of Goliath and shown it to the Philistines, struck them with panic and put them to flight, 1 Kings 17. The same did Judas Maccabaeus, cutting off the head of Nicanor and hanging it from the citadel of Zion, 2 Macc. 15:35. So too the Roman consuls M. Livius and Claudius Nero, having slain Hasdrubal with 56,000 Carthaginians and casting his head unexpectedly before the camp of his brother Hannibal, so struck him with consternation that he declared the fortune of Carthage was ended; and immediately fleeing he withdrew to a corner of Italy among the Bruttii, as Livy testifies at the end of book 26.

THEN ACHIOR, SEEING THE POWER (that is, so mighty, prodigious, and divine a victory over the slain Holofernes) WHICH THE GOD OF ISRAEL HAD WROUGHT (in Greek: of Israel, that is, of the Israelite people), HAVING LEFT THE RITE OF GENTILISM (idolatry and paganism), BELIEVED IN GOD, AND CIRCUMCISED (not by his own hand but by that of some Levite) THE FLESH OF HIS FORESKIN, having become a proselyte and a Jew. For although the Ammonites, Arabs, Phoenicians, Egyptians, and Ethiopians had long since received circumcision from Abraham and the Jews, they had received it as a free practice, not as binding and necessary, as the Hebrews had received it by God's command. Hence many did not circumcise themselves on account of the shame and pain of circumcision, and gradually circumcision had, as it were, faded away among them. Achior therefore, though an Ammonite, was uncircumcised, but converted to Judaism by the deed of Judith, he circumcised himself. For circumcision was the profession of Judaism and the Mosaic law, just as baptism is now the profession of Christianity and the Evangelical law.

AND HE WAS ADDED TO THE PEOPLE OF ISRAEL, so that he might have, as it were, the right of citizenship among the Israelites with respect to all their privileges, rights, dignities and even offices and magistracies. For although God had forbidden in Deuteronomy 23:3 that Ammonites and Moabites be admitted into the assembly of Israel, nevertheless He permitted dispensation from this law on account of the outstanding merits of some person toward Israel, such as those of Achior, who had publicly professed before Holofernes the providence and protection of God toward Israel, and had celebrated it with worthy praises. So Ruth the Moabitess was admitted into the assembly of Israel, and marrying Boaz became the ancestress of David and hence of Christ. For this was declared to be the mind of God by the legal experts, or Prophets, or Pontiffs consulting God through the Urim and Thummim, of which I have spoken at Exodus 28:28.

FOR NO ONE DARED TO OPEN THE BEDCHAMBER OF THE VALOR OF THE ASSYRIANS BY KNOCKING OR BY ENTERING. "Of valor," that is, of power and majesty — thus they call Holofernes their commander and prince. Just as we call a king or prince Royal Majesty, His Highness, His Excellency.

MICE COME OUT OF THEIR HOLES. Thus in contempt they call even the Jews, as though unarmed and unwarlike; but these mice they soon found had turned out to be lions, capable of overthrowing their elephants.
THEN VAGAO ENTERED HIS BEDCHAMBER AND STOOD BEFORE THE CURTAIN, AND CLAPPED HIS HANDS: for he supposed that Holofernes was sleeping with Judith.

As Tarquinius signified by lopping poppy-heads that the chiefs of the Gabini must be removed, so Judith by taking the head of the commander overthrew the whole army; for a camp without a leader is as a people without a king, sheep without a shepherd, disciples without a master.

So great was their terror that none spoke to his neighbor, but with heads bowed and all things abandoned they hastened to escape the Hebrews whom they heard coming upon them armed, fleeing through the roads of the plains and the paths of the hills.
The sons of Israel, seeing them flee, pursued them, descending with the sound of trumpets and shouts after them.
Because the Assyrians fled in scattered rout rather than in united formation, the sons of Israel pursuing in one body were able to cut down all they could overtake.
Ozias sent messengers through all the cities and regions of Israel to raise further forces for the pursuit.
Every region and every city sent their chosen armed youth after the Assyrians and pursued them with the edge of the sword until they reached the outermost borders of their own territory.
Those remaining in Bethulia entered the camp of the Assyrians and took away the plunder which the fleeing Assyrians had left behind, and were greatly laden.

Those who returned as victors to Bethulia brought back all that belonged to the Assyrians, so that there was no counting the flocks, beasts of burden, and all their moveable goods, and all from the least to the greatest were made rich from the spoils.
Joacim the high priest came from Jerusalem to Bethulia with all his elders to see Judith.

When she came out to him, all blessed her with one voice saying: Thou art the glory of Jerusalem, thou the joy of Israel, thou the honour of our people. # GEM The doctors apply this mystically to the Blessed Virgin, who by crushing the head of the infernal Dragon cast down all the armies of infernal spirits.

One Hebrew woman hath made confusion in the house of the king, for in slaying the prince she confounded, struck, and threw into disorder the whole camp. Note here the prudence of Judith, who sought the head of the commander; for with him removed she destroyed the entire army. # GEM This the doctors apply mystically to the Blessed Virgin, who by crushing the head of the infernal Dragon cast down all the hosts of his infernal spirits.

THEIR MIND AND COUNSEL FLED FROM THEM, AND AGITATED BY FEAR AND DREAD ALONE, THEY TOOK FLIGHT AS THEIR REFUGE — both because they saw their commander Holofernes beheaded, and because God took away their mind and counsel, so that, as men bereft of reason, they knew not what they should do. For otherwise, had they been in their right minds, they would have appointed another commander to force the few citizens of Bethulia to surrender; and also because God sent upon them a panic terror, so that with their leader slain, they thought themselves equally to be slain by the Hebrews. Wherefore they headlong gave themselves to flight; and thus fleeing, they were slain by a few pursuing and trumpeting Hebrews, just as a hundred thousand Midianites were slain by Gideon's three hundred soldiers, Judges 7.
THEY WEAKENED ALL — some by killing, others by wounding, others by maiming, etc. For the Hebrew word חלש (chalas) signifies all these things.

LADEN — that is, burdened with spoils. Some think it should be read 'honoured' (honestati), that is, enriched, as the Greek has it; for in Scripture 'honesty' often signifies riches, and 'to honour' is the same as 'to enrich.'

BUT JOAKIM THE HIGH PRIEST FROM JERUSALEM CAME TO BETHULIA WITH ALL HIS ELDERS (in Greek: with the Jerusalem senate, perhaps with the council of the Sanhedrin, which was the supreme council comprising seventy distinguished senators) "TO SEE JUDITH" — to bless her and congratulate her on so great a victory.

WHEN SHE HAD COME OUT TO HIM, THEY ALL BLESSED HER WITH ONE VOICE, SAYING: THOU ART THE GLORY OF JERUSALEM, THOU THE JOY OF ISRAEL, THOU THE HONOUR (honour and ornament) OF OUR PEOPLE. In Greek: Thou art the exaltation of Israel, thou the great exultation of Israel, thou the great glorying of our race. Vatablus: Thou the excellence of the Jerusalemites, thou the great joy of the Israelites, thou the illustrious glory of our race — as if to say: by liberating Jerusalem from Holofernes thou hast conferred great glory upon it; by defending the Israelites already worn out by fear and grief, thou hast instilled in them immense joy and a new life as it were; to all thy people thou hast brought a great name and honour among all nations, who hearing of this deed of thine will be astonished and say: so great a deed was wrought by a woman leader. # GEM Allegorically, Judith was a type of the Blessed Virgin. For she is the exultation, ornament, and glory not only of Jerusalem but of the whole world; not only of Israel but of all nations; not only of the Synagogue but of the whole Church. Hence St. Gregory Nazianzen in his tragedy Christ Suffering addresses her thus: Queen, Lady, blessing of the human race, be thou ever a friend to mortals, and in every place my greatest salvation. Cyril of Alexandria in his homily against Nestorius says: To thee be praise, holy Mother of God; for thou art the precious pearl of the whole world, thou the inextinguishable lamp, the crown of virginity, the sceptre of the orthodox faith. St. Chrysostom in a homily on the Mother of God salutes her thus: Hail, Mother, heaven, throne of our Church, its ornament, glory, and foundation. St. Ephrem in his sermon on the praises of the Virgin calls her the unique hope of the Fathers, the glory of the Prophets, the proclamation of the Apostles, the honour of the Martyrs, the joy of the Saints, and the light of the most eminent Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, the ornament of Aaron, the splendour of Moses, the fleece of Gideon, and the heaven of the Hierarchs, and the crown of all Saints and virgins, on account of her immense and inaccessible radiance and splendour. Damascene, oration 1 on the Nativity of the Blessed Virgin: O daughter worthy of God, ornament of women, glory of the human race, thou art the everlasting delight of the Patriarchs, the perennial exultation of the Prophets. # GEM

BECAUSE THOU HAST ACTED MANFULLY (as a virago, and a woman surpassing women, nay as a man surpassing all men) AND THY HEART HAS BEEN STRENGTHENED, IN THAT THOU HAST LOVED CHASTITY. Chastity therefore was the cause of Judith's fortitude, and this first, because chastity strengthens body and mind. Hence in olden times athletes about to compete in the Olympic games kept themselves entirely chaste, so as to bring unimpaired strength to the wrestling-ground, 1 Cor. 9:24. For lust dissipates and exhausts the noblest spirits, and therefore strength and vigour. Second, because God is accustomed to give this reward of fortitude to the chaste, and gave it wholly to Judith — fortitude, I say, both of body and mind for daring great things; for God, who is the purest spirit, singularly loves purity and chastity, and appoints the pure and chaste, as being like to himself, for great deeds. Hear St. Jerome in his preface to Judith: Receive Judith the widow as an example of chastity, and declare her with perpetual proclamations of triumphal praise. For he gave her as an example to be imitated not only by women but also by men — he who, as the rewarder of her chastity, granted her such virtue that she overcame one invincible to all men, and conquered the unconquerable. # GEM Third, because it was fitting that the lustful Holofernes should be overcome by the chaste Judith, so that chastity should triumph over lust. Hence Prudentius in the Psychomachia elegantly depicts chastity duelling with lust through Judith triumphing over Holofernes thus: After the Assyrian chamber and the neck of Holofernes, beheaded, bathed in blood soaked with lust; and rough Judith spurned the gem-studded bed of the adulterous commander, and restrained his raging passions with the sword: a woman bearing back a famous trophy from the foe with unshaking hand, my bold avenger from heaven. Moreover, the chastity of Judith was exceptional, and therefore she merited this exceptional reward and trophy. For she was a wealthy widow in the flower of youth, without children as will be clear in the following chapter verse 28, beautiful, honoured, and glorious on account of so great a victory; wherefore even all commanders and princes sought her as a bride. But she, loving chastity, despised all these things and remained single until death; and so she triumphed more nobly and gloriously over the flesh and the world than over Holofernes. Hear St. Ambrose, book on widows: Yet not elated by these successes — she who certainly might rejoice and exult by right of victory — she did not abandon the duty of widowhood; but despising all who sought her hand in marriage, she laid aside the garment of gladness and resumed that of widowhood; nor did she love the ornaments of her triumphs, judging those to be better by which the vices of the body, than those by which enemies are overcome. # GEM

AND ALL THE PEOPLES REJOICED WITH THE WOMEN AND VIRGINS AND YOUNG MEN, WITH ORGANS AND CITHARAS. The Greek has more fully: And all the women of Israel ran together to see her, and they made a chorus among themselves; and she took thyrsi in her hands, and gave them to the women who were with her, and they were adorned with olive, she herself and those with her.

Judith now sings this canticle to the Lord. This is the famous epinicion or victory hymn, analogous to the song of Moses (Exod. 15) and the song of Deborah (Judg. 5), sung after great deliverance from enemies.

# GEM Ambrose (De Virginibus) praises Judith's canticle as a model of chastity triumphing over lust and tyranny, saying she conquered by beauty of soul what armies could not conquer by strength of arms.

Begin to the Lord with timbrels, sing to the Lord with cymbals — the use of timbrels (tympana) recalls Miriam leading the women after the crossing of the Red Sea (Exod. 15:20), showing that women are especially apt instruments of divine praise after miraculous victories.
Sing a new psalm to Him — novum psalmum, a new canticle, because the victory is new and unprecedented; every new benefit of God calls forth a new song of praise, as Augustine says on Ps. 149.

The Lord who crushes wars, the Lord is His name — Dominus conterens bella. This is the ancient title of God as warrior and defender of Israel, echoing Exod. 15:3: "The Lord is a man of war, the Lord is His name." # GEM Origen notes that God crushes wars not by waging them but by making them unnecessary, breaking the pride of the aggressor.

Who placed His camp in the midst of His people — God Himself is portrayed as a general encamped among His soldiers, a reminder of the Ark of the Covenant dwelling in the midst of Israel, signifying His special providential protection.

Assur came from the mountains of the north in the multitude of his strength — the immensity of Holofernes' army is described to magnify the miracle of his defeat; his multitude stopped up rivers and his cavalry covered the valleys.

The mention of Assur coming from the north (ab Aquilone) is theologically significant: in prophetic literature the north represents the seat of evil and invasion, as in Jer. 1:14 and Ezek. 38:15, so Holofernes embodies the archetype of the enemy of God's people.

He said he would burn my borders and kill my young men with the sword, give my infants as prey and my virgins into captivity — Holofernes threatened the complete destruction of Israel: fire, sword, captivity. This threefold threat heightens the drama of God's singular deliverance through one woman.

But the Lord Almighty has struck him and delivered him into the hands of a woman, and she pierced him — tradidit eum in manus feminae. # GEM Jerome marvels: God chose a widow, adorned only with fasting and prayer, to do what no general or giant could do, showing that divine power works through human weakness, that "the foolish things of the world God has chosen to confound the mighty" (1 Cor. 1:27).

For the mighty one did not fall by young men, nor did the sons of the Titans strike him, nor did lofty giants oppose him — filii Titan. The Titans were the ancient giants of pagan mythology, symbols of supreme human power and martial prowess. Their failure to overcome Holofernes while a woman succeeded magnifies the divine paradox.

But Judith daughter of Merari by the beauty of her countenance dissolved him — in specie faciei suae dissolvit eum. Lapide notes that Judith used her beauty as an instrument of divine justice; beauty, which is often a snare to its possessor and to others, here becomes an instrument of salvation, just as the serpent's form was used against the serpent in the bronze serpent of Moses.
# GEM Chrysostom observes that God often confounds the proud through the very means they despise; Holofernes despised Israel's God and lusted after a woman, and by that very lust he was destroyed.

She put off the garments of her widowhood and put on the garments of joy for the exultation of the children of Israel — Exuit vestimento viduitatis et induit vestimento laetitiae. This change of garments is deeply symbolic: the widow's mourning is transformed into a nation's rejoicing. Lapide notes this prefigures the Church, clothed in mourning during her earthly pilgrimage, who shall one day put on the garments of eternal glory.

The thyrsus mentioned in the prologue — the ivy-wreathed staff of Bacchus carried by victors — is aptly applied to Judith, who slew the drunken Holofernes as a new Bacchus and thus deserved the thyrsian crown which she had wrested from the very hands of the drunken enemy. This is an erudite comparison: as Virgil (Ecl. 5) writes of thyrsi woven with soft leaves, so Judith's victory wreath was won from the Bacchic revel of her enemy.

THEN JUDITH SANG THIS CANTICLE TO THE LORD, SAYING: BEGIN (sing, play, and shout for joy) TO THE LORD.] Hence, with her leading, the others responded: just as, with Miriam the sister of Moses leading, the other women responded after the crossing of the Red Sea, Exodus 15:20. For the Greek text reads here: But Judith led this song of thanksgiving among all the Israelites, and the whole people sang this praise with a loud voice, and Judith began: Begin to God with timbrels, etc. From this it is clear that Judith was prophetic, indeed a writer of Holy Scripture, for she composed this canticle, which is a part of Holy Scripture, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

THE LORD CRUSHING WARS.] She alludes to the name Jehovah, which, derived from the root הוה (hava), means the same as "crushing." She cites it from the triumphal song of Moses, Exodus 15:3: The Lord as a man of war, Almighty is his name. See what is said there.

ASSUR CAME (that is, the Assyrian army sent by Nebuchadnezzar king of the Assyrians under Holofernes) FROM THE MOUNTAINS FROM THE NORTH.] For Assyria lies partly to the East of Judea, and partly and more so to the North. WHOSE MULTITUDE BLOCKED THE TORRENTS, AND THEIR HORSES COVERED THE VALLEYS.] Sanchez, understanding Nebuchadnezzar who sent Holofernes to mean Xerxes, applies these things fittingly to him. For Xerxes led such great forces into Greece that rivers are said to have been barely sufficient for drinking, lands for passage, and seas for sailing. So Paul

[Commentary on the army of Holofernes] Herodotus in book 7 and Justin in book 2 hand down accounts of his army; concerning which likewise Juvenal in Satire 10 says: "We believe that the lofty spiders failed, and that the rivers were drunk dry by the Mede as he marched." But Xerxes was a Persian, not an Assyrian. See what has been said in the preface.
[NOCUIT EUM] That is, it was formerly used to harm him; for "noceo" took the accusative, as is clear from Plautus and others.

[Nec filii Titan — that is, lofty giants, as he explains in what follows — percusserunt eum.] You will ask who the sons of Titan are, or as it is in Greek, of the Titans? I answer: Titan or Titanus is invented by the Poets as the son of Coelus and Vesta, that is, of Heaven and Earth, and the Titans are their sons, concerning whom Virgil writes in the sixth Aeneid: "Here the ancient race of Earth, the Titanian youth, hurled down by lightning, roll in the lowest depths." Earth, says Servius, first begot the Titans against Saturn, then Giants against Jupiter. And the fable goes that the Titans were created by Earth, enraged against the Gods, for her own vengeance. Hence the Titans were also called from τίσις, that is, from vengeance. For Jupiter cast them down into Tartarus. They say the Titans were six brothers and five sisters, called after their mother Titea, as Diodorus attests; of whom one was Iapetus, father of Prometheus. Hyperion also is of the number of the Titans, who is said to have first observed and demonstrated the movements of the sun, moon, and stars, and is therefore called the parent of the sun and stars. Hence the Sun is called Titan by the Poets, the Moon Titania, Diana Titania. Cicero in his translation of Aratus says: "When Titan clothed him with perpetual light." Ovid in the Metamorphoses: "While Titania there bathes in the accustomed water." Titan therefore is an epithet of the Sun. For the Sun is called the son of heaven and earth, because in the morning it seems to be born and rise from the earth and sea, and likewise as the son of Jupiter it shines in heaven as in its father's house. Hence many derive Titan from the Hebrew טיט, that is, mud, slime, earth, so that from Tit is derived Tethys and Titea, the mother of the six Titans. Others derive Titan from τιταίνω, because Titan, that is the Sun, extends his rays in every direction. Furthermore the Poets fable that Titea is the earth from which the sun and moon came forth. For the earth had various names. For as M. Varro says, cited by Saint Augustine in book 7 of the City of God, earth or tellus was called Ops, because it is made better by help and cultivation; and Mother, because it brings forth very many things; and Alma, because it provides food; and Proserpina, because crops creep forth (proserpant) from it; and Vesta, because it is clothed (vestiatur) with herbs, or, as others say, because it stands by its own power. Hence there survives a Hymn to the Titans in Orpheus. # GEM From this, in a second sense, by sons of Titan one might understand Phaethon with his offspring, whom the Poets say was the son of the sun; who, while incautiously driving the horses and chariot of the sun, burned everything on earth with the heat of the sun — as if to say: it was not the sun or Phaethon that struck Holofernes, but the woman Judith.

In a third sense, some think that by Titans, as sons of heaven and earth, are signified spirits and winds (likewise storms and thunderbolts), which, sent down from heaven, that is from the air, into the earth, are hidden within it, from the verb τιταίνω, that is, I extend, because winds extending their hands and acting with force do injury; from which earthquakes arise by which many perish — as if to say: not winds, not storms, not thunderbolts, not earthquakes, but one woman alone laid low Holofernes. This is supported by what Pausanias writes in his account of Corinth, that on the hill of the Titans near Sicyon there was an altar of the winds placed there, at which a priest sacrificed each year to appease the wildness of the winds. The first sense, as it is the more common, so it is the more fitting: for the Titans indeed...

They were most mighty giants, whom no one was able to resist. Hence the proverbs: 'A Titanic aspect,' that is, grim, fierce and terrible. 'More ancient than the Titans and Cronus (time and Saturn), thou callest upon the Titans.' See Apollodorus at the beginning of book 1 on the Origin of the Gods, Hesiod in the Theogony, Giraldus and others.

St. Gregory, *Moralia* bk. 9, ch. 6. The translators both of the Seventy and of the Vulgate [lit. "ours"] occasionally render Hebrew names, which among them are called by other terms, by the vocabulary of the fables of the Greek and Latin Gentiles, into the Greek and Latin tongue — not as though they narrate or approve those fables; but because we do not well nor rightly understand what is said except through those words which we have learned by use and, as it were, imbibed with our mother's milk, as St. Jerome says — especially when the fables draw their origin from Sacred Scripture, as happens here. For the fable of the Titans arises from these his-[torical accounts...]

[On the sons of Titan/giants] The Septuagint renders "cornu subii" as "cornu Amaltheae" in Job 42:14, which poets feign to be a goat that nursed Jupiter with her milk. Similarly in Lamentations 4:3, our version renders "lamias" where the Hebrew has תַּנִּים (Tannium), a word signifying dragons or empty and terrifying monsters, such as lamias are. The same judgment applies to Arcturus, Orion, and similar names mentioned in Job and the Prophets, as St. Jerome says, of whom the poet writes: "Arcturus, and the rainy Hyades, and the twin Triones, and Orion armed with gold she beholds." Therefore the sons of Titan or of the Titans are sons of giants, that is, very vast, very strong and robust giants, who are called Rephaim, for which the Septuagint renders Titans in 2 Samuel 6:18. It is a metonymy: for by the character and strength of the fathers is signified the character and strength of the sons. Therefore by Titans in common popular usage none other are understood than giants.

[Further on Titans] It could also be said that this word Titan was in use among the Assyrians, Chaldeans, Hebrews, and other peoples through fables spread throughout the entire world for signifying giants, so as to indicate that Holofernes and the Assyrians, proud of their strength and forces, compared themselves to Titans, and said they were equal to the Titans, or feared nothing save the Titans: so that something Titanic was constantly heard spoken of in their camp, to which Judith alludes, saying: the Assyrians fell not by the hand of their own Titans, but by my hand, who am a weak and unarmed woman. So Sanchez.

HORRUERE PERSAE CONSTANTIAM EJUS, ET MEDI AUDACIAM EJUS — "The Persians were horrified at his constancy, and the Medes at his boldness." Hence it is clear that among the Assyrians in the camp of Holofernes Persians and Medes were mingled; especially because the general Holofernes himself was a Persian, says Cedrenus; and the Medes, after Arphaxad their prince was defeated, had already been subjugated by Nebuchadnezzar, to whom therefore many also from the neighbouring Persians had defected on account of the idleness and shameful rout and flight of their king Xerxes from Greece, as I said in the Prooemium.

TUNC ULULAVERUNT CASTRA ASSYRIORUM, QUANDO APPARUERUNT HUMILES MEI ARESCENTES SITI — "Then the camp of the Assyrians howled, when my humble ones appeared, perishing with thirst." That is, my fellow citizens of Bethulia, nearly killed by thirst, when after Holofernes was slain by me, they invaded his camp and pursued the fleeing Assyrians.

FILII PUELLARUM — "Sons of maidens," that is, small boys or young men: for those whose mothers are still maidens and young women cannot be of greater or more adult age. Hence it is clear that youths and boys together with men rushed upon the fleeing Assyrians.
COMPUNAVERUNT EOS — "They pierced them" with their knives and small swords.

MISISTI SPIRITUM TUUM ET CREATA SUNT — "Thou didst send forth thy spirit and they were created." Understand spirit of the mouth, that is, the word and command by which God in Genesis 1 said and commanded: Let there be sun, moon, birds, fish, etc., and immediately they were made. It alludes to Psalm 32:6: By the word of the Lord the heavens were established, and by the spirit of his mouth all their power.

MONTES A FUNDAMENTIS MOVERENTUR — "The mountains would be moved from their foundations." When God should command, as if to say: So great is the power of God that by his mere nod he can transfer mountains and melt rocks; even so now he has overcome the camp of the Assyrians, which appeared unconquerable like high mountains and hard rocks, through me a woman, and has scattered it like wax melting away.

DABIT ENIM IGNEM ET VERMEM IN CARNES EORUM UT URANTUR ET SENTIANT IN SEMPITERNUM — "For he will give fire and worms into their flesh, that they may burn and feel torment forever." # GEM Hence St. Jerome, Chrysostom, Augustine, Theophylact, Haymo, Lyra, Abulensis, Dionysius, the Glossa, Ribera, Maldonatus, Serarius and others probably judge that in hell there are bodily worms that gnaw the flesh of the damned, just as there is a true fire burning them: see what was said on Ecclesiasticus 7:19, and ch. 10:13, and the last verse of Isaiah 24.

MOX UT PURIFICATI SUNT — "As soon as they were purified" from the uncleanness and legal irregularity contracted from the slaughter of the Assyrians and contact with the dead, by washing their bodies and garments, according to the law of Numbers 31:24.

ANATHEMA OBLIVIONIS — "The anathema of oblivion." The Hebrew חֵרֶם (cherem) signifies anathema, that is, a gift torn away from profane uses, entirely separated and consecrated to God. The phrase "of oblivion" is not in the Greek; hence some think it should be read "of oblation," but all Latin manuscripts have "of oblivion," and this fits the passage aptly: for it is called the anathema of oblivion because it was dedicated to God to ward off oblivion of so great a benefit and victory among posterity. So Serarius. Or because it brought oblivion of the past famine, thirst, and thousand afflictions which the Hebrews had previously suffered from the Assyrians: so Sanchez. Thus Joseph named his son Manasseh, that is, oblivion, saying: The Lord hath made me forget all my toils, Genesis 41:51; and Isaiah ch. 65:16: The former afflictions are delivered to oblivion. On which passage Jerome excellently says: They will forget former evils, not by forgetfulness of memory, but by the succession of good things, according to what is written: In the day of good things there is forgetfulness of evils.

SECUNDUM FACIEM SANCTORUM — "Before the face of the saints," that is, before the temple or before the Synagogue: for this served as a temple for those dwelling outside Jerusalem. For in it they prayed together and heard the law of God.

MANSIT AUTEM IN DOMO VIRI SUI ANNOS CENTUM QUINQUE — "But she remained in the house of her husband a hundred and five years," that is, until the years of her age were one hundred and five. By a similar phrase the Hebrews are said to have dwelt in Egypt four hundred and thirty years, that is, until the four hundred and thirtieth year from the departure of Abraham out of Chaldea: for otherwise the Hebrews dwelt in Egypt only two hundred and fifteen years, Exodus 12:40. See what was said there. Hence those are less correctly of the opinion that Judith, after she became a widow, lived one hundred and five years; for thus at her death she would have been one hundred and forty years old or more, which was unheard of at that time.

The Greek adds that Judith, before she died, divided her property among her own relatives and those of her husband. From this it is clear that she had no children. All the more admirable was her chastity. Let Christian princes imitate Judith—those who, lest their family line fail, change their resolution, indeed their vow of celibacy, and take wives in order to have children as heirs and continuators of the family. Judith did not act thus; rather she preferred celibacy to all children. For chastity is nobler than all these things. Indeed she was mindful of that divine promise made to eunuchs who had castrated themselves of their own will, out of love of God and chastity: "I will give them in my house and within my walls a place and a name better than sons and daughters; I will give them an everlasting name that shall not be cut off" (Isaiah 56:5).

AND ALL THE PEOPLE MOURNED HER FOR SEVEN DAYS.] Judith is held to be a saint by the Fathers. But the day of her death is not assigned in the Martyrology, because it is unknown. Nevertheless the Ethiopian Church celebrates the feast of Judith on the fourth day of the sixth month, which is called Elul, as Serarius states.

BUT THE DAY OF VICTORY.] In the Hebrew Calendar, Judith is assigned to the 25th of the ninth month, which is called Casleu, on which day likewise the memory of the giving of fire and of the Dedication (Encaenia) of the Maccabees is commemorated, as Genebrard, Torniel, Sigonio, and Salianus attest—the last of whom assigns this eulogy, as it were an epitaph, to Judith: "This is that Judith who converted the honeyed eloquence of her tongue, the angelic dignity of her countenance, her flashing eyes, the brightness of her brow, the crimson of her cheeks, her coral lips, and the majesty of her whole person into arms and arrows, by which barbarian fury might be captured and powerless might laid low. This is that Judith who, though she was a prodigy of beauty, was nonetheless a miracle of chastity, which she cultivated and cherished with fasts, prayers, and every affliction of the body; so that, while she flourished in the prime of life, lacked children, abounded in riches and glory, she shone celibate and undefiled for seventy-five years. She lived seventy-two years after the pride of Holofernes was laid low and the empire of the Babylonians shaken, and reached in all her hundred and fifth year."

ALLEGORY. Judith represents the Blessed Virgin, who frees cities and the faithful devoted to her from the siege of enemies, as she freed Constantinople, dedicated and devoted to her, in the year of the Lord 717, from a three-year siege by the Saracens, when Saint Germanus, Patriarch of Constantinople, presided over it. Writing to him accordingly, Pope Gregory II, in his fourth letter, says: "The wondrous deeds of that holy Warrior and Lady of all Christians, which were manifested in her, through which you too were preserved among enemies, must not be passed over in silence. For when they raged with much madness and tumult, they found in her as much hostile adversity as the injustice of their cause deserved. Nor is this to be wondered at: for if Bethulia was saved for the Israelites through the hands of Judith, whose work was the removal of Holofernes, and was on that account called in those times the salvation of Israel, how was it not fitting that your holiness also, using such a champion, should prevail over the enemies of the city, and crown her with victory," etc.

But most especially the Blessed Virgin, as the true Judith, defends Bethulia, which in Hebrew signifies virginity. For she herself, being the Virgin of virgins, protects virgins as it were her own clients, as she has shown by very many examples in every age, and in particular she of old preserved virgins condemned by tyrants to the brothel (which was the supreme torment for them: for they preferred, as Tertullian says in the Apology, to be condemned to the lion than to the pimp) undefiled through Angels or lions; as she preserved Saint Agnes, Saint Daria, Saint Lucia: so that you will scarcely find any of them to have been violated. Therefore, when you feel the temptations of the flesh, invoke the Blessed Virgin, and you will emerge victorious. # GEM

Finally, how lovable and admirable Judith was to posterity and all ages is clear from the fact that heroines and queens coveted the name of Judith and, with the name, the imitation of her virtue. Thus the wife of the Emperor Louis the Pious, who was the son and successor of Charlemagne, was Judith Augusta, who was banished by her adversaries—and especially by Pippin, the son of Louis by his first wife Irmingarde—to the monastery of Saint Radegunde, where she took the monastic veil upon herself; but she was restored to empire together with her husband Louis by Pope Gregory IV, as Baronius relates after Marianus Scotus, in the year of Christ [820].
Judith was likewise the name of the daughter of Charles the Bald, King of the Franks, who, marrying the King of the English, went to England in the year of the Lord 855, as Baronius attests.

A third Judith was Queen of Poland, of whom Baronius writes thus after Longinus, vol. 11, at the end of the year of Christ 1007: "In this same year Judith, Queen of Poland, a most holy woman, wife of King Boleslaus and his helper in promoting piety, departed this life, to the great grief of all, to reign forever with the saints." Thus Longinus concerning the time, who also recounts the deeds nobly accomplished by her.

A fourth Judith was the daughter of Henry, Emperor, and Queen of Hungary in the year of Christ 1074, expelled from the kingdom by injustice; Pope Gregory VII consoled her with letters addressed to her, in which he praises her heroic virtue. The letters survive in Baronius at the end of the year of Christ 1074.
A fifth Judith was the wife of Count Hermann, a woman of holy life, who migrated from this life to the heavenly one in the year of the Lord 1005, as Baronius writes.
Judith, and by crasis Jutta, a sixth, was the daughter of the Duke of Saxony, who married the son of the King of Denmark Eric, as is clear from letter 112 of Pope Gregory IX.
Judith, or Jutta, a seventh, was a virgin handmaid of Saint Elizabeth, who was renowned for miracles both in life and after death.

Judith, or Jutta, an eighth, was a virgin of Parthen, whose virtues and miracles Abraham Bzovius recounts from the Chronicles of Poland at the year of the Lord 1260, on which she died; where among other things he says: "At last, as the end of her life drew near, seized by illness (in which she often repeated these words: 'O how much does adversity of health, and misery, and poverty profit those who trust in the Lord, for obtaining anything from God!') she saw Christ the Lord appearing to her, three times, with many Angels and other Saints, who invited her to himself with these words: 'Come now, my handmaid, into my kingdom.'"
